content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-11 Daily Overview What was done: Deep technical work across three areas: academic research, robot learning, and development tools: constructing a methodology for writing NeurIPS papers (based on 61 real reviews), fully implementing the BetterSSH multi-server management system, refactoring the MimicGen augmentation architecture for error recovery, optimizing the development experience in the entire workspace How it was done: Through parallel multi-device collaboration (MacBook for academic research + ASR integration, Tianhe server robot experiments, TzJsDesktop full-stack development), using superpowers skill chains (brainstorming→ccplan→optimize), ECL documentation-driven adversarial review, and multiple rounds of iterative debugging Impact: Produced a reusable academic writing framework (5 mental models + 10 heuristics), a production-level SSH terminal redeveloper (195 tests passed), fixed data quality issues in MimicGen (metadata completion + quality threshold setting), and standardized development configurations for 13 projects MacBook What was done: Three parallel deep sessions: construction of NeurIPS writing skills (breakthroughs in OpenReview API + analysis of 61 reviews), expansion of MeetingHelper’s streaming ASR backend (Moonshine v2 + Voxtral), batch configuration of Claude Code permissions for the algorithms directory, creation of error recovery documentation, and debugging of MimicGen augmentation failures (6 rounds of iteration) How it was done: Web crawler + API authentication + review data mining (544 papers → 7 oral presentations → 61 reviews), research on cutting-edge ASR libraries + Python API verification, analysis of directory structures + generation of unified configurations, analysis of code bases to generate CLAUDE.md and TUTORIAL.md, synchronization of code from Tianhe and running SpaceMouse demonstrations Impact: Complete academic writing framework (reviewer psychology analysis + chapter-level guidelines), MeetingHelper supports two new real-time ASR models (73ms latency), improved development experience for 13 projects, complete project documentation (covering 12 error skills), identification of all known MimicGen bugs (subtask fragmentation, gripper detection, path parsing) DCC What was done: Review of MIHD project progress How it was done: Document analysis and task prioritization (P0/P1/P2) Impact: Clarified the execution paths for 3 P0 blocking tasks, 4 P1 core tasks, and 3 P2 enhancement tasks TzJsDesktop What was done: BetterSSH full-stack project (from zero to production), standardization of workspace permissions, implementation of TokenMonitor using a data archiving system How it was done: Superpowers skill chain (brainstorming requirement analysis → ccplan ECL-driven implementation → code optimization), parallel execution of 5 subagents for independent modules, tool-level wildcarded permission rules, time boundary partitioning to achieve deduplication archiving Impact: Production-ready SSH terminal redeveloper (Node.js+Fastify+React+xterm.js, 195 tests passed), reduction of 13 configuration files from 100 lines to 30 lines, establishment of a usage-archive layer to prevent data loss, fixing bugs in pricing cache version control Tianhe What was done: Resolving git proxy issues and synchronizing 42 files in the gadget repository, refactoring the MimicGen recovery augmentation system architecture, repackaging the portable collection How it was done: Diagnosing proxy configuration mismatches and using per-command overrides, creating ErrorSceneVariantGenerator to implement reset-based variants, introducing augmentable-only quota rules, completing MimicGen metadata, running pytest unit tests Impact: Synchronized latest translations in the cluster development environment, aligning augmentation processes with original paper designs, fixing issues with success_demos.hdf5 that cannot be augmented again (5/5 verification passed), exposing human demo data quality issues (24 issues with only 3 replayable, 0 augmentable) A highly intensive technical workday across four devices: completed BetterSSH full-stack project from zero to production, optimized multi-project development tools, deeply refactored MimicGen augmentation system, constructed a NeurIPS academic writing framework, expanded speech recognition backend
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ BetterSSH Full-Stack Implementation (Zero to Production) — Completed the entire BetterSSH lifecycle: requirement analysis (unified management of 2-5 server AI sessions), architecture design (Node.js+Fastify+ssh2/node-pty+React+xterm.js), ECL documentation + adversarial review (fixed 9 defects including 2 critical security issues), implementation of 41 files (monorepo + dual WebSocket + dual-mode parser), 8 code optimizations (reconnection/verification/DRY/performance), server selection panel, comprehensive upgrade of terminal components (WebGL renderer+Unicode11+Catppuccin theme+ResizeObserver+instance pooling) ✅ NeurIPS 2025 Dataset Track Writing Skill Construction — Constructed a complete writing skill based on 7 oral presentations + 56 Spotlight papers: retrieved 544 papers, identified top-tier papers, broke through OpenReview API 403 blockages (requires authentication token), obtained 61 official reviews, analyzed review psychology (rating distribution, pros and cons keywords), extracted writing patterns from 3 ArXiv full-text papers, created SKILL.md (453 lines, 8 chapters) including 5 mental models, 10 decision heuristics, review insights, chapter-level writing guidelines ✅ MimicGen Recovery Augmentation System Refactor — Refactored augmentation strategy from ‘repetitive error injection’ to ‘fixed error scene + reset sampler’: created ErrorSceneVariantGenerator, improved subtask segmentation logic (6-subtask simplified to 2-subtask), fixed gripper detection bugs (find continuous closure rather than first-time threshold exceedance), implemented multi-process parallel verification, fixed path parsing bugs, added ensure_env_interface_attrs to complete metadata, introduced augmentable-only quota rules ✅ TokenMonitor Usage Data Archive System — Built a persistent archiving layer with hourly aggregation (time boundary deduplication): archive.rs core module + parser integration + background loop trigger + SSH device support, applied 5 optimizations (extracting frontier checks, boundary security, let…else syntax, set预分配集合), fixed pricing cache version control bugs (adding CACHE_VERSION=2 to make outdated caches obsolete) ✅ Optimize Claude Code Permissions for Entire Workspace — Systematically optimized permissions configuration for all projects in the GitHub workspace: used tool-level wildcards “Bash” instead of 80+ item-by-item enumerations, changed deletion operations from prohibited to required confirmation, overwrote 13 configuration files (7 main projects + 5 gadget subprojects + root directory), created init-claude.sh automation script ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark Documentation — Created complete CLAUDE.md and TUTORIAL.md: system architecture, error classification methods (12 Error Skills × D0/D1), 5 RBG groups, detailed descriptions of approximately 50 modules, retained existing SpaceMouse operation guides ✅ MeetingHelper Streaming ASR Backend Expansion — Integrated two new ASR backends: (1) Moonshine v2 (34-245M parameters, 73-107ms latency, real-time English speech, blocks for other 7 languages) (2) Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime (4B parameters, 4-bit quantization to ~3.1GB, automatic language detection, Apple Silicon MLX acceleration). Modified live-subtitle.py, start.sh, health-check.sh, verified API correctness through source code inspection Implementation & Fixes ✅ Claude Code Permission Configuration Batch Setup — Created unified.claude/settings.json for the algorithms directory (root + 7 first-level projects + 5 gadget subprojects): automatically allowed Read/Edit/Write/Glob/Grep/Bash/Agent/WebFetch/Skill; only asked about destructive operations (rm, git clean, git reset –hard, git push –force) 🔄 MIHD Project Progress Review — Reviewed MIHD plans.md to extract remaining work: identified P0 blocking tasks (HD GPU jobs, published baseline data collection, DE analysis pipeline), P1 core tasks (in-sample query verification, multi-method query comparison, cross-section evaluation for expansion, bootstrap CI), P2 enhancement tasks (HD tissue boundary biology, cross-section alignment evaluation, supplementary charts) Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. All MimicGen augmentation verifications failed (0/10 scenes), cubeA not lifted (height 0.82 desktop) Solution: Multiple rounds of debugging revealed the root cause: subtask fragmentation (6 subtasks = 5 independent warp boundaries, cumulative errors) + no post-grasp buffer + gripper detection bug (find continuous closure rather than first-time threshold exceedance) + retract subtask not warp + path parsing bug. Fixed issues one by one: simplified to 2-subtask, added 15-frame buffer, improved gripper detection, merged retract into re_acquire, fixed path parsing
Key Insight: Original MimicGen design wisdom: minimize the number of subtasks (2) + post-grasp buffer + start from home position. Each subtask boundary is a source of error, so should be minimized as much as possible
2. success_demos.hdf5 could not serve as MimicGen source — lacked env_interface_name and env_interface_type attributes Solution: Added ensure_env_interface attrs() helper to automatically complete attributes in filter_valid_source, augment_success_demos, generate_success_from_error; patched HDF5 files for 7 tasks
Key Insight: MimicGen DataGenerator relies on specific datagen_info attributes to identify environment interfaces. Missing these attributes causes get_env_interface_info_from_dataset failure; integrity must be ensured at each output point in the generation chain
3. BetterSSH front-end buttons were unresponsive — WebSocket messages were silently discarded Solution: Traced back to null return of onMessage() placeholder in index.ts. Instantiated SessionManager, loaded SSH/BetterSSH configurations, connected to the complete message route (get_sessions, create_session, destroy_session, send_input, resize)
Key Insight: The backend had a fully implemented SessionManager (500+ LOC) but never instantiated it — a code skeleton trap, interfaces existed but never connected
4. Token usage data disappeared when deleting JSONL logs Solution: Implemented hourly append archiving under {app_data_dir}/usage-archive/, using time boundary partitioning (archive covered [0..frontier], live source covered (frontier..now)) to prevent duplicate counting
Key Insight: Time boundary partitioning eliminated overlap between archiving and real-time data, ensuring zero duplicate statistics with minimal storage overhead (about 1KB/day/provider)
5. Claude Code frequently requested permission confirmation in exploratory projects, affecting development efficiency Solution: Simplified the 80+ item-by-item enumeration Bash command permissions into a tool-level wildcard “Bash”, covering all Bash commands
Key Insight: Claude Code supported tool-level wildcards (“Bash” matching all Bash commands), allowing relaxed permissions with minimal configuration. Dangerous operations were still subject to confirmation mechanisms through an ask array
6. Adversarial review found WebSocket PTY writes without authentication (ATK-007) Solution: Added NEWREQ-009: binding to 127.0.0.1 + 256-bit session token (randomly generated and printed at startup), all WS upgrades required a query string token
Key Insight: Security issues were least costly to fix during the requirements phase. Red-blue confrontation should be enforced in Phase 8 (after architecture is determined, before coding starts)
7. OpenReview API returned 403 Forbidden, preventing access to NeurIPS paper review data Solution: Diagnosed double-layer blockages: (1) OpenReview API now forced all requests to require a authentication token (anonymous access disabled), (2) Frequent probing triggered nginx rate limits. Used the user OpenReview credentials (tongtongtot@163.com) to log in through openreview-py SDK, successfully retrieved 61 reviews (7 oral papers: 29 reviews + 7 AC decisions; 8 Spotlight papers: 32 reviews). Added delays between requests to avoid rate limiting
Key Insight: OpenReview’s authentication strategy had changed — even public content API access required a login token. The /login endpoint remained accessible (returned a 400 verification error) while all data endpoints returned 403, indicating that application-level authentication was enforced. Web front-end work used internal credentials due to Next.js RSC server-side rendering
8. Terminal rendering quality was poor — fonts were blurry, scrolling was slow, middle button pasting failed Solution: Identified xterm.js as the industry standard (used by VS Code/Replit), and the problem was lack of plugins. Added WebGL renderer (GPU acceleration), Unicode11 (CJK), improved theme (Catppuccin Mocha), better font stack (Cascadia Code fallback)
Key Insight: xterm.js alone was insufficient — production terminals required a complete plugin ecosystem. WebGL provided a 10-fold rendering improvement and clear fonts. Unicode11 prevented CJK width errors
9. GLM5.1 and DeepSeek-Reasoner displayed a price of $0.00, despite the OpenRouter integration being added Solution: Added version field to the PricingCache structure (CACHE_VERSION=2), making outdated caches (only containing LiteLLM data) automatically obsolete when new pricing sources were introduced
Key Insight: The caching system needed explicit version control to handle mode evolution — only relying on TTL allowed old data to exist after code changes
10. Terminal session switching discarded all terminal content — no rollback history Solution: Changed the terminal lifecycle: created an xterm instance once in useEffect([], []) and reused across sessions. When switching: term.clear() → subscribe to new session → backend sent RingBuffer回填 → term.write()
Key Insight: Terminal instances were expensive and required saving rollback states — should not be destroyed when switching sessions. The 2MB backend RingBuffer provided a replay buffer
General Issues 11. Tianhe cluster git pull failed, reporting ‘GnuTLS recv error (-110): The TLS connection was non-properly terminated’Solution: The proxy configuration is incorrect: the global Git settings point to 172.16.31.200:3138, while the environment variable (from proxy_on) uses 127.0.0.1:10087. Using the git -c http.proxy=http://127.0.0.1:10087 -c httpsproxy=http://127.0.0.1:10087 pull command overrides the proxy settings for each command, successfully pulling the repository. Key Insight: Git has two independent proxy configuration layers (global settings vs environment variables) that may conflict. Using the -c flag to override the settings for each command provides an immediate solution without modifying the persistent configuration.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MimicGen Augmentation Strategy — Repeated error injection vs Fixed Error Scene + Reset Randomization Role Approach Human Humans identify the fundamental problems in the current logic: Repeating error injection for each target scene results in lack of diversity, which does not conform to the original MimicGen design. An error scene should be treated as a fixed base scenario, and variants should be generated by randomizing objects using env.reset() AI Initially, AI only sees the details of the code implementation (_replay_with_mimicgen_targets), without questioning the design from the perspective of the augmentation strategy. It is only after humans point out the problems that AI understands that the reset logic from the original MimicGen should be moved to the error scene Difference Analysis: Humans consider “what is a reasonable way to augment” from the systematic design level, while AI tends to accept the existing implementation and optimize within its framework. This represents a difference at the strategic vs tactical level.
Whether to Research Existing Solutions Role Approach Human After completing the design presentation, humans proactively suggest “I need you to do some research to see if there are any ready-made or useful code examples”, avoiding reinventing the wheel AI Initially, AI is prepared to go directly to the implementation stage (writing a design document → initiating writing plans), without actively suggesting research Difference Analysis: Humans demonstrate engineering pragmatism (reusing is better than rewriting), while AI tends to fall into the habit of “reinventing from scratch” when not prompted. This is one of AI’s blind spots.
Clarifying the Scope of NeurIPS Writing Skills Role Approach Human After initial exploration, users clarify: “I want to refine the writing methodology—how they complete the writing and how they present highlights. I hope to output a nuwa-skill format that helps me write papers for the NeurIPS 2026 dataset track.” They specifically request focusing only on Oral/Highlight papers (not all 544 papers) AI Initially, AI interprets the request as refining the paper topic/topic. After clarification, it shifts to analyzing the writing structure (chapter organization), narrative strategy (narrative tension), review psychology (review psychology), and contribution presentation mode (highlight presentation) Difference Analysis: Users have a clear meta-cognitive goal (learning the writing process itself), while AI initially focuses on domain knowledge. The nuwa-skill approach correctly abstracts at the right level—extracting cognitive frameworks rather than summarizing facts. This highlights the importance of instructional norms for complex creative tasks.
Local Session Support (Not Initially Considered) Role Approach Human During the design process, humans suddenly realize that “in addition to remote SSH, I will also use Claude and codex cli locally. I want to include this part as well” AI Initially, AI is designed only to cover remote SSH sessions. It is not expanded to a type: local Difference Analysis: Humans discover an implicit requirement (local session) during the design process, while AI fails to infer that including a local scenario is also necessary from “I am very reluctant to switch between different windows now”. AI needs stronger demand-digging capabilities.
Simplification Strategy for Permission Configuration Role Approach Human Humans recognize that frequent confirmation is the main friction point in exploratory projects and propose retaining only the confirmation requirement for deletion operations AI AI provides a technical implementation: Using tool-level wildcards “Bash” to replace item-by-item enumeration, changing the deletion command from deny to ask Difference Analysis: Users define requirements and boundaries (exploratory projects vs production projects, deletion operations require confirmation). AI provides technical implementation and optimization suggestions (using tool-level wildcards).
Decision on Archive Scope Role Approach Human Choosing “local + SSH remote device”, although SSH already has a remote-cache persistence mechanism, it is preferred to prioritize additional security in the unified archive layer AI Recommending “only local” to avoid duplication, as SSH already has a remote-cache mechanism Difference Analysis: Humans value redundancy and consistency (all data stored in a single archive system) over technical efficiency (avoiding duplicate mechanisms).
Judgment of Terminal UX Quality Role Approach Human Intuitive evaluation based on intuition: “It doesn’t look like a terminal at all, something feels wrong” — using real terminals for immediate pattern recognition AI Technical checklist: Identifying missing WebGL renderers, Unicode11, appropriate themes, resize synchronization — the system diagnoses the root cause Difference Analysis: Humans are good at evaluating overall UX, while AI is good at identifying technical gaps. Humans immediately identify problems, and AI provides diagnosis and prescriptions.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Did not proactively suggest research: After AI’s design was completed, it was ready to go directly, without suggesting “research existing solutions first”. This requires human reminder. Engineers should have the awareness that “reusing is better than rewriting”. Insufficient demand-digging: When users say “unwilling to switch windows”, AI only focuses on remote SSH scenarios and fails to infer that local CLI also needs unified management. Strong scenario integrity reasoning is needed. It took 6 rounds of diagnosis to locate all MimicGen bugs: Subtask fragmentation → retract not warping → gripper detecting errors → insufficient interpolation steps → parallel path bugs → cache cleanup. AI cannot anticipate all problems at once. Failed to access the OpenReview API upon first attempt, without realizing recent authentication policy changes. All 30+ attempts (API v1/v2, different endpoints, visitor login, various headers) returned a 403, until the user provided credentials. The system lacks the latest knowledge of OpenReview authentication requirements for 2026. Failed to detect that SessionManager was never instantiated during initial architecture scanning — it took a lot of time to debug port conflicts and WebSocket issues before the root cause was discovered to be an empty onMessage handler. AI failed to question the rationality of “repeated error injection” design when analyzing, and required humans to explicitly point out “fixed error scene + reset randomization” to start refactoring. Incorrect path assumption: The Agent initially assumed that files were edited under recovery_collection_portable/, but users had removed one level. All edits were lost and needed to be re-applied in the new path. Did not initially recognize that an outdated pricing cache could prevent OpenRouter data loading — it was only discovered through explicit code tracing using litellm.rs cache logic that there was a version control gap. General Limitations Triggered OpenReview nginx rate limiting, resulting in temporary IP bans (152.3.43.42) after approximately 30 consecutive requests during the diagnosis phase. Exponential backoff or diagnostic authentication requirements should have been implemented earlier. Subagent execution process is not transparent: When 5 backend agents are running concurrently, users cannot see intermediate progress (only “waiting for completion notification”), lacking real-time visibility. Progress awareness is a UX pain point for large tasks. Learnings Key Learnings The power of the Superpowers skill chain: Brainstorming (requirement analysis) → ccplan (ECL-driven implementation) → optimize (quality improvement) forms a complete software lifecycle. ECL documents serve as cross-session persistent “design contracts”, ensuring context consistency across multiple rounds of dialogue, multiple agents, and even different models. The value of adversarial review: Among the 9 issues discovered by the Critic agent, 2 are CRITICAL (no authentication PTY writing, SSH parameter injection) and 4 are HIGH (token brute-force cracking, prototype contamination), which could cause security disasters if exposed in a production environment. Repairs at the requirement stage cost « code stage « production stage. The essence of object-centric warping: EEF relative to object motion patterns is an invariant. This invariant is maintained through coordinate transformation T_eef_new = T_obj_target × T_obj_src⁻¹ × T_eef_source. Two-step understanding: (1) Eliminate the absolute position of the source object, (2) Remap with the new object’s position. The wisdom behind the original MimicGen design: (1) Minimize the number of subtasks (only 2 for Stack), (2) Use a post-grasp buffer of 10-20 frames to ensure stable capture, (3) Reduce pose gaps by using env.reset() to home position, (4) Execute in a quasi-closed-loop manner (calculate delta for target_pose_to_action step by step). The core design of MimicGen is source demo + env.reset() randomization: An error scene should be treated as a fixed “initial state”, and then variants should be generated using placement_initializer, rather than injecting errors repeatedly for each target. Priority of NeurIPS DB Track reviews (based on 61 reviews): The most valued qualities are “comprehensive” (mentioned 17 times), “community” (17 times), “novel” (15 times), “clear” (28 times), and “well-designed” (13 times). The most common criticisms are “lack of comparison” (insufficient baseline), “insufficient detail” (unclear methodology), and “limited scope” (restricted application). The best paper (Artificial Hivemind) has an average score of 4.75 for Oral papers — this exceeds the review scores determined by AC. The average for Oral is 5.00 < Spotlight average is 5.16, confirming that scoring is not the only factor considered. Parallel agent scheduling mode: Assign independent modules (Config/Parser/LocalPTY) to different subagents for parallel implementation, then converge and verify, improving development efficiency by 3-5 times. The key is identifying non-dependent leaves in the DAG, maximizing parallelism. Claude Code –output-format stream-json: Through research, it was found that Claude natively supports structured output (newline-separated JSON), eliminating the need for regular parsing of PTYs. This improves the reliability of the Parser from “heuristic” to “deterministic”. Always research before reinventing. Return on investment for TypeScript strict mode: 195 tests + 0 type errors = production-ready code. The type system is particularly effective when refactoring (after extracting parsed-state-utils, all calling points automatically get type checked). Spending 20% more time on typing in the early stages saves 80% of debugging time later. xterm.js is the industry-standard browser terminal (used by VS Code, Replit, Vercel, ttyd). Its quality depends on a complete plugin stack: WebGL for GPU rendering, Unicode11 for CJK, Fit for resizing, SearchAddon, WebLinksAddon. Just the basic xterm.js is not sufficient for production use. The MimicGen-ready dataset requires a specific metadata structure: Each demo_*/datagen_info must contain env_interface_name and env_interface_type, otherwise DataGenerator cannot initialize the environment correctly. Human demonstration data quality verification should be carried out before the collection phase: Simply having teleop success is not enough; replay stability and augmentability must also be verified. Otherwise, a large amount of data that cannot be used for training will be collected. Time boundary partitioning (archive completion hours, live source coverage current hours) elegantly solves duplicate removal without requiring explicit trace at the record level — zero overlap means zero duplication count. Claude Code’s permission system supports tool-level wildcards (such as “Bash” matching all Bash commands), allowing for minimal configuration instead of dozens of item-by-item rules. The cost of Subtask fragmentation: Each subtask boundary is an independent “check object pose → re-warp → 5-step interpolation”, and errors will accumulate. 6 subtasks = 5 error sources, far more than the original one. OpenReview API authentication architecture (2026): All data endpoints require login with a token; only the /login endpoint allows anonymous access (returning a verification error). The web frontend uses server-side credentials through Next.js RSC. Rate limiting is operated at the nginx level (based on IP), triggered in a short time after approximately 30 requests. The Academic Papers Review API is becoming increasingly locked down, requiring institutional or personal credentials. Terminal resize synchronization requires full-chain coordination: ResizeObserver (frontend) → term.onResize → WebSocket message → SessionManager → Pty.resize(). Mismatches lead to errors in the error column. Damping is needed to prevent flooding when resizing with drag-and-drop. Terminal instances should not be destroyed during session switching — they retain a rollback buffer (10,000 lines). The correct pattern: Create once and backfill with clear() + backend RingBuffer (2MB) for cross-session reuse. A cache system must include explicit version fields when the data source can evolve — just TTL expiration is insufficient to handle changes in patterns/sources. The permission configuration allow/deny/ask three-tier mechanism can balance security and efficiency: Allow implements automation, ask retains confirmation for dangerous operations, deny is used for absolute prohibition. Claude Code configuration does not automatically inherit (each project requires independent configuration), but a standardized initialization process can be implemented through an automated script (such as init-claude.sh). Complex robot system documentation should distinguish between developers and operators: Clearly define the target audience, structure, language, and content retention through structured questions to prevent ambiguity. React closure trap ref pattern: Registering third-party library callbacks in useEffect for xterm.js onData captures expired props is a common bug. The general solution is: props → ref, useEffect synchronizes with ref.current, and the callback reads ref.current. This is the best practice in the React ecosystem. Three key elements for WebSocket robustness: Redial (exponential backoff), backpressure (checking bufferedAmount), and heartbeat (not implemented but should be considered). The network layer of distributed systems must assume that “connections may disconnect at any time”, not “connections should remain stable”. The importance of continuous closure detection: Robot capture may have multiple attempts. If gripper_closed > threshold for the first attempt fails, it must detect continuous closure (≥10 frames) to confirm a successful capture. Complexity of multi-process debugging: (1) Each worker requires an independent env+augmenter instance, (2) Path resolution is more error-prone (relative paths are likely to miss levels), (3) Absolute paths like demos_dir must be explicitly passed in, (4) Robosuite WARNING needs to be suppressed separately in the worker function. Quota counting logic should reflect actual availability: augmentable_only is stricter but more reasonable than replay-only, as a non-augmentable demo cannot expand the training set even if it can be replayed. State of the art ASR models (2026): True streaming models are rare. Moonshine v2 (73-107 ms latency, 34-245 M parameters) and Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime (480 ms, 4B parameters) represent the most advanced. Moonshine uses a callback-based API suitable for real-time transcription; Voxtral requires manual chunking but offers higher accuracy. Both support Apple Silicon native acceleration (Moonshine uses CoreML, Voxtral uses MLX). Permission requirements differ between exploratory projects and production projects: Exploratory projects prioritize efficiency (lenient permissions), while production projects prioritize security (strict confirmation). Gradually accumulating configurations can lead to inconsistencies (e.g., 80+ long rules, different project styles), but regularly using templates for standardization can improve maintainability. Practical Learnings Git proxy configuration operates at two independent levels: global configuration (git config --global http.proxy) and environment variables (http_proxy, HTTP_PROXY). Mismatches lead to cryptic TLS errors; using the -c flag to override the settings for each command provides an immediate solution while preserving existing configurations.✅ Building NeurIPS 2025 DB Track Paper Writing Skills (nuwa-skill Format)
19:50:53.430 | claude_code
The user wants to use the nuwa-skill framework to refine the paper writing methodology for the NeurIPS 2025 dataset track, including feedback from OpenReview reviews. The AI conducted multi-stage research: (1) Scraped the NeurIPS virtual site, identifying 7 Oral papers and 56 Spotlight papers among approximately 544 DB Track papers; (2) Encountered an OpenReview API 403 error, diagnosed as authentication requirements and rate limits; (3) With the user providing credentials, the AI successfully retrieved reviews for 61 papers (29 Oral, 32 Spotlight); (4) Conducted in-depth analysis of review psychology (scoring patterns, pros and cons keywords); (5) Studied the full-text structures of 3 ArXiv papers; (6) Created a complete SKILL.md document (453 lines) with 5 mental models, 10 decision heuristics, review insights, and chapter-level guidelines. Six reference documents containing original review JSONs were created. Key findings: The best paper received the lowest score (4.75), Oral papers had average scores lower than Spotlight papers—AC judgment surpassed scores. MeetingHelper ✅ Integrating Moonshine v2 and Voxtral Streaming ASR Backends
02:13:58.742 | claude_code
The user requested the addition of a Whisper streaming model (previous sessions completed), followed by a request to search for other streaming ASR options. The AI researched and recommended Moonshine v2 (extremely lightweight, 73ms latency) and Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime (最高准确性, 4B parameters). The user approved both options. The AI verified the APIs through source code inspection, installed dependencies (moonshine-voice, voxmlx), implemented MoonshineTranscriber and VoxtralTranscriber classes, and updated start.sh/health-check.sh. Final verification: AST parsing was correct, shell syntax was valid, imports were successful.
algorithms ✅ Configuring Algorithms Directory Permissions for Claude Code
04:36:45.682 | claude_code
The user noticed that Claude Code frequently prompted during the exploration of the algorithms folder. They requested creating a local setup to skip unnecessary confirmations and asked the AI to traverse subdirectories to identify complete projects. The AI explored the directory structure and found 13 projects (7 first-level, 5 gadget sub-projects). A unified .claude/settings.json was created for all projects: automatically allowing Read/Edit/Write/Glob/Grep/Bash/Agent/WebFetch/Skill; only asking about destructive operations (rm, git reset –hard, git push –force). All 13 files were verified to be created successfully.
MIHD 🔍 Project Progress Review and Task Priorities
05:55:20.759 | claude_code
The user asked what else needed to be done based on the plans.md document. The AI read a 36K token planning document and extracted a priority task list: 3 P0 blocking tasks (HD GPU operations, published baseline data, DE analysis pipeline), 4 P1 core tasks (query verification, method comparison, cross-verification, bootstrap CI), and 3 P2 enhancements. The presented verification list showed that none of the items were selected. The user did not choose any next actions during this session.
Error Recovery Benchmark ✅ Creating CLAUDE.md and Updating TUTORIAL.md Completely
08:18:03.956 | claude_code
The user started the init command to create a CLAUDE.md guide file for the error recovery bot code library. After completion, they requested an update to the TUTORIAL.md covering all files. The Agent used ccplan skills to clarify requirements through AskUserQuestion (target audience, structure, language), confirming that the target readers were new members and operators, while retaining existing content in Chinese. A detailed document was generated, including system architecture, 12 error skills classification methods, 5 RBG groups, a complete file list (approximately 50 modules across frameworks/configs/scripts directories), SpaceMouse operation guidelines, and an explanation of the augmentation pipeline. The user expressed impatience during the generation process but accepted the final deliverable.
gadget ✅ Using Proxy Configuration for Git Pull in Bug Fixing
07:09:09.908 | claude_code
The user requested enabling a proxy (proxy_on) for git pull on the tianhe cluster. The initial attempt failed due to a TLS connection error. The Agent diagnosed that the proxy configuration did not match: the global git configuration used an outdated 172.16.31.200:3138, while the environment variables pointed to 127.0.0.1:10087. Using the git -c flag overwrote the proxy settings for each command, successfully pulling 42 files, including new Ollama translation support (common/ollama.py, common/translation.py), bilingual website functionality (website/translate_site_batch.py), and test suite additions. It was recommended to update the global git proxy configuration to match the environment variables to maintain consistency in the future.
Error Recovery MimicGen 🔄 Running Collection Script and Diagnosing Augmentation Failures
04:18:28.819 | claude_code
The user ran the stack task collection script, with human operations successful but validation failures (0/10 scenes). The Agent added a diagnostic mode and modified 5 return None points to return diagnostic information. Running again revealed that cubeA was not lifted (0.82 desktop height), resulting in re_grasp failures. The issue was traced to subtask fragmentation problems.
🔄 Reconstructing Subtask Segmentation Logic and Fixing Gripper Detection Bugs
04:18:28.819 | claude_code
The original MimicGen method was rewritten to reduce RBG_A/B/D from 6-subtasks to 2-subtasks, with post_grasp_buffer set to 15 frames. Four files were modified (recovery_types.py, recovery_mimicgen.py, recovery_segmenter.py, recovery_collection.yaml). Running the test still failed, revealing a gripper detection bug (finding the first closure instead of continuous closures), which was fixed but still resulted in failures.
✅ Understanding MimicGen Detection Mechanism and Object-Centric Warping Principles
04:18:28.819 | claude_code
The user asked how mimicgen detection was implemented. The Agent explained 4 core aspects (task phase detection, scene stability, source demonstration verification, object reference parsing). Then, the warping mathematical principles were explained, along with why data could be augmented. The user raised questions about the source of scenario variations, and the Agent clarified that the entire scenario was different (not just one object). Finally, the user requested a comparison with the original MimicGen.
✅ Researching the Original MimicGen and Proposing New Architectures
04:18:28.819 | claude_code
The Agent dispatched two research agents to search for papers online and read source code locally. It was found that the original Stack had only 2 subtasks, with scenario sources being randomized by env.reset(). The user proposed a new idea: using error scenarios as a base for applying the original randomization logic. The Agent verified the feasibility and analyzed the advantages and disadvantages. Then, the latest code from tianhe was downloaded and decompressed to update the system.
✅ Implementing Multiprocessing Parallelization and Fixing Path Bugs
04:18:28.819 | claude_code
The Agent implemented parallel verification using ProcessPoolExecutor (10 workers), with each worker creating an independent env+augmenter. After running, all 10 workers threw a ValueError. The issue was traced to a path parsing bug (scenes_dir.parent.parent missing one layer), which was fixed by adding the demos_dir parameter. At the same time, warnings from robosuite were suppressed (creating macros_private.py, modifying mimicgen init.py, and deleting .pyc caches).
error_recovery_benchmark ✅ Reconstructing MimicGen Augmentation System + Improving Human-Driven Collection Quality
04:46:32.733 | codex
Humans pointed out current mimicgen logic issues (repetitive error injection instead of fixed scenarios + reset randomization). The AI implemented architectural restructuring: creating ErrorSceneVariantGenerator to implement reset-based variants, and modifying recovery augmentation and validation logic. During the verification phase, two key issues were identified: (1) success_demos.hdf5 lacked metadata required for MimicGen, preventing secondary augmentation; (2) the existing 24 human demos had insufficient quality (3/24 replayable, 0/augmentable). The AI fixed the metadata issue (adding ensure_env_interface_attrs), improved the collection process (introducing augmentable_only quota + settle gate), and repackaged the portable collection (197M). 11 unit tests were completed to verify the correctness of the new logic.
GitHub Workspace Configuration ✅ Optimizing Exploratory Projects for Claude Code Permissions
05:57:02.260 | claude_code
Due to the exploratory nature of the project, the user requested simplifying the permissions confirmation for the entire GitHub workspace. The AI systematically optimized 13.claude/settings.local.json files across 7 main projects and 5 gadget sub-projects, replacing over 80 individual Bash command permissions with a single “Bash” wildcard. Deletion operations were moved from “deny” to “ask”. The init-claude.sh script was created to standardize future project initialization. The configuration file was reduced from approximately 100 lines per project to about 30 lines.
BetterSSH ✅ Designing and Implementing BetterSSH from Scratch: Managing AI Agent CLI Sessions on SSH Servers
04:08:26.234 | claude_code
After the user raised the request, the AI used brainstorming skills to clarify the requirements through 9 structured questions (2-5 servers, real-time output + status + alerts, direct interaction, cross-platform). Three technical solutions were proposed, and a Node.js+web progressive architecture was recommended. Seven design modules were presented section by section (overall architecture, SSH configuration, Session management, dual-mode parser, front-end UI, project structure), and the user confirmed their acceptance. The user actively requested technical research, and the AI identified Claude stream-json support and Glissa 3-tier detection strategies. Using ccplan skills, an ECL document was created, and 9 defects were fixed through adversarial review (including 2 CRITICAL security issues). Phase 10 implementation started with 5 parallel subagents, completing 41 TS files, 195 tests, and pushing to GitHub. Finally, the optimize skill was used to implement 8 code optimizations (WebSocket reconnection, type verification, DRY refactoring, etc.), all passed validation (195/195 tests + 0 type errors).
✅ Implementing Server Selection Panel via ccplan
2026-04-11 (Session 2) | claude_code
The user wanted two changes: prioritizing the terminal over the Claude window and enabling SSH server selection (not just local). The ccplan workflow was executed. Phase 0: Calibration to a pop-up server selector. Phase 1-9: Designing 6 file reconstructions (adding local to servers list in the backend, adding useSessionStore.servers, useControlWs.get_servers, new ServerPicker component in the frontend). All changes were implemented, and code review identified 4 HIGH issues (outdated closures, lack of a11y). All issues were fixed. The service was started for verification, and the user requested to close it.
✅ Terminal Component Fully Upgraded to Production Quality
2026-04-11 (Session 2 continued) | claude_code
The user reported that the terminal “looked bad, not like a terminal.” The issue was diagnosed: lack of WebGL (slow, blurry), no Unicode11 (CJK corruption), terminal destruction during session switching (loss of rollback), no resize synchronization (line break corruption). An alternative solution was identified—confirming xterm.js was industry-standard (used by VS Code, Replit). Implementation: installing the unicode11 plugin, rewriting Terminal.tsx with WebGL + Catppuccin theme + ResizeObserver + instance pooling, adding a message type from resize to end-to-end, and backend RingBuffer subscription for backfilling. Type checking passed, service successfully launched.
🔄 Initial Project Onboarding and Debugging Sessions
07:20:07.628 | claude_code
The user asked how BetterSSH could be used (a unified Claude Code/Codex session manager). The architecture was explained (Fastify backend with dual WebSocket, React frontend using xterm.js). Attempting to launch resulted in “button not responding.” The root cause was identified: SessionManager never instantiated, and onMessage returned null. The ESM import issue (ssh-config LineType) was fixed, and the SessionManager was connected to the WebSocket handler. The user interrupted before completion.
✅ Service Restart and Verification
07:35:35.274 | claude_code
The user requested a clean restart of both the frontend and backend. Old processes (PID 6016, 7300) were killed, and both services were successfully restarted. The backend was verified on 127.0.0.1:18790, and the frontend on localhost:5173. The user confirmed that the service was running and requested to close it.
TokenMonitor ✅ Implementing usage-data-archive Using ccplan Workflow
07:58:31.978 | claude_code
The user requested a data persistence layer to prevent JSONL logs from being lost during deletion. The ccplan workflow was executed: calibrating intentions (local + SSH device hourly aggregation), generating solutions (archive-primary with time-bound partitions). The user approved, and the archive.rs core module, parser.rs integration, background loop triggering, and SSH device support were implemented. All 321 tests passed.
✅ Diagnosing and Fixing GLM/DeepSeek Pricing Display Bugs
07:41:45.881 | claude_code
The user reported GLM5.1 and DeepSeek-Reasoner displaying $0 despite OpenRouter support. The pricing lookup chain was traced through pricing.rs/litellm.rs/openrouter.rs, revealing that the underlying issue was the PricingCache lacking a version field, allowing 7-day old caches (before OpenRouter) to remain valid and preventing new data from being applied. A version field with CACHE_VERSION=2 was added to automatically invalidate old caches on the next startup.
✅ Using /optimize Skills to Optimize Archive Implementation
07:58:31.978 | claude_code
After compression, the newly implemented archive function was tested using the /optimize skill. 5 optimizations were applied: extracting frontier checks into ArchiveFrontier::is_up_to_date() (eliminating approximately 40 lines of duplication), adding boundary checks for string slicing, refactoring 3 match patterns to let…else, adding a timestamp-based error log for failed parsing, and pre-allocating collections. All 325 tests passed with zero clippy warnings.

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W14 (2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05) This week, three main tasks were carried out simultaneously: preparation for the MIHD multi-modal spatial transcriptomics paper (5 days, DCC); completion of the TEDDY full-embedding benchmark, Leiden comparison experiments, HD fusion evaluation, and writing of a bilingual paper framework. The research focus was established as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with differentiated studies. Additionally, systematic fixes were made to TokenMonitor Linux FloatBall over 5 days. After multiple iterations, the issue evolved from symptom-based fixes to a fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构. All 21 code review issues in PR #4 were also resolved. The Life-Copilot Discord Bot was completely reimplemented over 2 days, resulting in a net reduction of 2823 lines of code. A multi-CLI agent architecture was introduced, and new features such as screen time tracking, Lark input channels, and MCP tool integration were implemented. Furthermore, research on 63 papers from the NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track and setup of a new Linux workstation environment were completed.
Weekly Overview Indicator Value Date Range 2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05 Active Days 7 / 7 Total Conversations 29 Projects Involved 12 Tasks Completed 44 Ongoing Tasks 9 Total Tokens 328,660,991 Total Cost $218.53 Claude Code Tokens 273,046,863 Claude Code Costs $189.80 Codex Tokens 55,614,128 Codex Costs $28.73 Average Daily Cost $31.22 Project Progress MIHD Multi-modal Spatial Transcriptomics Paper (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completed extraction of TEDDY-G 400M full-embedding data for 11 slices and multi-strategy evaluations. It was confirmed that the STAIG framework is highly robust for gene encoders (Teddy ARI=0.495 vs PCA ARI=0.497). Integrated Leiden clustering (binary search matching n_clusters) and STHD pathology GT, completed full-Leiden comparison on DLPFC (9/9 groups). The key conclusion was that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods (STAIG+mclust=0.546 vs STAIG+Leiden=0.513). Completed evaluation of 13 HD fusion strategies. Key negative findings included: simple fusion (concat ARI=0.191) being inferior to single-modal PCA (ARI=0.351), and adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (ARI=0.003). Established the core narrative framework for the paper (Setting=No system study on zero-shot FM embedding fusion/Finding=Fusion Strategies > Simple Fusion is a Trap, Spatial Graph Learning is Key/Application=Cross-slice Retrieval). A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods. Created 4 GPU Slurm job scripts and 3 paper analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). The docs/ folder was reorganized, and 12 expired scripts were archived. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Basic contrastive HD fusion experiment is still running (48h limit). The HD learning-based fusion result set is not complete. ⚠️ The root cause of the 151676 slice STAIG model collapse has not been thoroughly analyzed. It is currently handled by skipping it. TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Adaptation (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Fixed Wayland positioning issues in the Linux main window (forcing GDK_BACKEND=x11 to fallback to XWayland). Completed all 21 code review fixes in PR #4: assert!() → Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries, path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions. There were zero Rust warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. Identified the root cause of GTK size negotiation issues (WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective). This was resolved by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, and scroll bars were fixed. Completed the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构: The window permanently maintained an expanded size. Cairo::Region was used to control the click-through area. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Organized git history (git reset –soft compressed 24 commits into 2 semantic commits) and pushed it to GitHub. A new Linux workstation version v0.6.1 was built (deb/rpm/AppImage). Blocked Issues:
⚠️ The fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution needs to be verified on actual devices. ⚠️ There are architectural constraints regarding Linux WM clamping. The correct solution is to fix it with a fixed large size and internal CSS. This needs to be fully verified. Life-Copilot Discord Bot (2 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completely reimplemented 3170 lines of complex intent routing into a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server (19 tools) + Skills (4). A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. Implemented Screen Time Tracker (GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade, AI application classifier, independent asyncio.Task sampling loop, 26/26 tests passed). Integrated Lark Bot for quick idea capture (daemon thread asyncio bridge solved lark-oapi blocking issues, 10 categories of automatic classification). Added Codex/Anthropic backend. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Fixed two overlapping problems in MCP tool calls: Claude -p requires –mcp-config and –permission-mode bypassPermissions. Added structlog structured logging + complete dialogue JSONL audit. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Accessing OpenReview reviewers’ comments is blocked due to a general 403 error in the API. The OPENREVIEW_USERNAME/OPENREVIEW_PASSWORD environment variables are required. New Linux Workstation Environment Setup (2 active days) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Completed the full system initialization (NVIDIA driver confirmation, Miniconda, SSH key migration of 7 files, TinyProxy proxy, GitHub SSH verification). Verified that RTX 5090 has BF16=220 TFLOPS/s. Configured gadget rclone for two-way synchronization (gdrive remote, covering four types of data: summarize/research/website/test). Installed Claude Code skills and 12 plugins. Research on NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track (1 active day) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Analyzed 63 papers under the gadget research scout framework. Deeply analyzed 5 top papers, generating a three-dimensional report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions: multi-dimensional evaluation benchmark for VLM credibility, TTA and fine-tuning synergistic effect benchmarks, online continuous learning timing benchmarks. Key Tasks ✅ TokenMonitor PR #4 All 21 Code Review Fixes (April 02) — System fixes were carried out according to CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM priorities: changing assert!() to Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries (Clone released in advance), path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions, NaiveDate overflow. There were zero Rust compilation warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot Complete Reimplementation (April 04) — Replaced the complex intent routing with a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server + Skills. Supports Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI fallback chains. The Skills JSON protocol triggers Discord UI decoupling. A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. ✅ TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux GTK Size Negotiation Fix (April 04) — Identified the root cause of WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective. Resolved this issue by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor expansion direction misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, GTK ScrolledWindow scroll bars, and two-stage collapse were fixed. All tests passed. 🔄 TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Fixed-Window + GDK Input Shape Architecture Reimplementation (April 05) — Reorganized the float ball from a dynamic resize architecture to a fixed expanded-size window + GDK input_shape_combine_region control for click-through. Eliminated the systematic WM +305px offset. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Waiting for final runtime verification. ✅ MIHD Paper Narrative Line Organization and Bilingual Framework Creation (April 03) — Established the research focus as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with a three-layer narrative structure: Simple Fusion is a Trap → Spatial Graph Learning is the Bridge → Correct Fusion Transcends Single Modal. A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods (including Abstract/Introduction/Results R1-R6/Discussion/Methods). ✅ Leiden vs KMeans Full-Embedding Comparison Experiments (April 01) — Completed comparison of Leiden clustering on 9/9 combinations. The key conclusion was that STAIG+mclust(0.546) > STAIG+Leiden(0.513). Leiden significantly improved weak embeddings (+29%), but had little additional benefit for strong embeddings (+6%). This established the core argument that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods. ✅ MIHD Fig 1-6 Framework Planning (March 31) — Referred to MISO, STAIG, QueST, and Yuan’s papers to plan Fig1=framework, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=Ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=Cross-slice Query, and Fig6=Query bio-discovery. ECL YAML and plans.md were written. ✅ Life-Copilot Screen Time Tracker Full Implementation (April 04) — Planned a 9-stage ccplan + Critic adversarial review (pre-detected Wayland compatibility risks). Implemented GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade window tracking. AI application classifier (100+ pre-settings + persistent cache). Independent asyncio.Task sampling loop. Passed 26/26 tests. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot MCP Tool Call Fixes (April 05) — Fixed two overlapping issues: Claude -p requires explicit –mcp-config to load project-level MCP configuration; non-interactive mode requires –permission-mode bypassPermissions. The –allowedTools wildcard was unreliable due to competition with MCP server initialization. By running the Claude -p command directly, the root cause of permission_denials field issues was identified. ✅ NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track Systematic Paper Research (April 04) — Searched for 63 papers and deeply analyzed 5 top papers (TTA-VLM, DATE-LM, CAB, KG-RAG Pitfalls, DCAD-2000). Generated a three-dimensional analysis report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions. ✅ Teddy + STAIG Fusion Evaluation (Full 11 Slices) (March 30) — Completed evaluation of 10/11 slices (skipped model collapse on 151676 models). Avg ARI=0.495 compared to PCA+STAIG’s 0.497. On NMI, Teddy won 6 out of 10 slices. This revealed the high robustness of the STAIG framework for gene encoders, providing experimental evidence for future encoder replacement strategies. ✅ Life-Copilot Lark Bot + Codex/Anthropic Backend + Full-Linkage Token Verification (April 04) — Implemented ThoughtCaptureService shared service layer + AutoCategorizer (10 categories: Chinese & English) + daemon thread asyncio bridge. Added CodexProvider and AnthropicProvider. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Created key_validator.py to verify all services with real APIs. Fixed Google Calendar scope mismatch issues. ✅ FEAT-003: STHD Ground Truth Integration (March 31) — Added a load_sthd_annotations() function to automatically load 545913 annotations. Combined CE and Tumor (Neoplasm+Non-neoplastic Epithelium→Epithelium). 17502/17502 spots had 100% match rate. 3 valid clusters were identified, providing a GT basis for HD ARI/NMI evaluation. ✅ TokenMonitor Linux Main Window Wayland Positioning Fix (April 02) — Identified the Wayland protocol-level prohibition of client-side absolute position settings. After detecting WAYLAND_DISPLAY in the run() entry, forced GDK_BACKEND=x11 to make the application run via XWayland. 283 Rust tests passed. ✅ MIHD GPU Slurm Scripts and Paper Analysis Scripts Batch Creation (April 02) — Created 4 independent Slurm GPU job scripts (STAIG/contrastive/LLaVA/AdaLN) and 3 Python analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, paper figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). All scripts passed syntax verification. ✅ Life-Copilot Structured Logging and Operation Audit System (April 05) — Replaced stdlib logging with structlog. Implemented dev-color output + prod JSON format + 10MB rotation. Created audit_log.py to write complete dialogue JSONL audits in Discord on_message, FastAPI middleware, and CLISelector.send. Fixed ExtraAdder omission issues. 420 tests passed. ✅ TEDDY-G 400M Full-Embedding Extraction (11 Slices) (March 30) — Extracted 1024d gene embeddings for DLPFC’s 11 slices in the conda teddy environment and cached them in embeddings_cache/gene/teddy/. Total size was 185MB. Also extracted UNI2 staig_strict visual features (256×256 patch, no ImageNet normalization, frequency-domain filtering). 🔄 HD Learning-Based Fusion GPU Experiments (April 01) — staig(ARI=0.258)/llava(ARI=0.201)/adaln(ARI=0.003) were completed. The basic_contrastive experiment is still running (48h limit). Key negative findings included: adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (17502 spots), revealing that data size is a key variable in fusion methods. ✅ docs/ Folder Reorganization and Code Gap Analysis (April 03) — Archived 5 expired documents. Created a three-level structure in docs/paper/figures/. Systemically analyzed the capabilities required by draft_en.md vs existing code. Archived 12 expired scripts. Planned Leiden result recovery scripts, figure generation scripts, spatial continuity metrics, and support for 4 code tasks with multiple seeds. Issues and Solutions 1. GTK bottom-up size negotiation caused FloatBall windows to always be 400x400. Tauri’s set_size() was completely ineffective [TokenMonitor] (April 04)### Next Week’s PlansNext week’s key focus areas are as follows: (1) MIHD paper – Collect the complete HD evaluation table for basic_contrastive HD experiments and advance the code gap tasks (Leiden result restoration script, figure generation script, spatial continuity metrics, multi-seed support). Start generating papers and writing drafts to reach a submission-ready level.
(2) TokenMonitor – Finalize the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution on real Linux devices. Confirm that the WM clamp issue has been fully resolved. If verification is successful, submit a PR for merging. Close this long-term task related to Linux FloatBall fixes after nearly two weeks.
(3) Life-Copilot – Obtain OpenReview account credentials to unlock access to NeurIPS review comments. Verify the stability of Screen Time Tracker in production environments. Continuously monitor the performance of the 11-level fallback chain in real-world usage.
(4) Research exploration – Translate the research conclusions from NeurIPS D&B Track into specific experimental designs. Evaluate the feasibility of a multi-dimensional benchmark for assessing VLM credibility. Also follow up on the progress of the AionUI remote SSH access feature request.

summary: 本周有三条任务同时推进：MIHD多模态空间转录组论文准备工作（5天，DCC负责）已经完成，包括TEDDY全量嵌入基准测试、Leiden对比实验、HD融合评估以及论文双语框架撰写，从而确定了“零样本FM嵌入融合”差异化研究的方向；TokenMonitor Linux FloatBall系统的修复工作也已完成，经过多轮迭代，从症状修复发展到固定窗口+GDK Input Shape架构重构，同时解决了PR #4中全部21个代码审查问题；Life-Copilot Discord Bot的重构工作也完成了，删除了2823行代码，引入了多CLI Agent架构，并实现了屏幕时间追踪、飞书输入渠道、MCP工具集成等多项新功能。此外，还完成了NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track中63篇论文的调研工作，以及新的Linux工作站环境的搭建。
title: Weekly Summary 2026-W14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-05 Key Points of the Day “Completed Work: Reorganized Linux FloatBall from a dynamic sizing architecture to a fixed-size window layout. Implemented GDK Input Shape solution for Life-Copilot Discord bot. Additionally, added a structured logging system called structlog to address issues with missing MCP tool configurations and permission denied errors in Claude CLI -p mode.” “Implementation Method: For FloatBall, ccplan generated six alternative solutions, which were then implemented using Cairo::Region and GTK geometry_hints for fixed window layouts. During dragging operations, the input shape temporarily expands to cover the entire window. For Life-Copilot, the system analyzed nested process call chains to resolve permission denied errors in non-interactive mode.” “Achievements: Eliminated systematic position offset caused by Linux WM +305px/+113px layout issues and removed approximately 150 lines of debugging/compensation code. The complete conversation history of the Discord bot was stored locally, and the Google Calendar MCP tool now operates perfectly in production environments.” By restructuring Linux FloatBall with fixed windows and GDK Input Shape, we effectively resolved platform-level geometric problems. Additionally, we addressed two main issues: lack of structured logging for Life-Copilot Discord bot and permission denied errors when using MCP tools in non-interactive mode.
Tasks for Today Architecture and Strategy “🔄 TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Architecture Reorganization (Fixed Window + GDK Input Shape) — After generating six alternative solutions using ccplan, a fixed window layout was implemented. The window size always remains 152×56. GDK input_shape_combineRegion is used to control the click area: only the ball area is displayed when folded, while the entire window is shown when unfolded or during dragging. Three issues were resolved: ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures. The snap function was removed to ensure stable movement. Currently, there are 284 lines of Rust code and 205 frontend tests passed, with final verification in progress.” “✅ Complete Fixes to Life-Copilot Discord Bot’s MCP Tool Calls — Two-stage fixes were implemented: In the first stage, it was discovered that Claude -p would not automatically load project-level.claude/mcp_servers.json files. The –mcp-config parameter was added to the claude_adapter.py command to load 19 MCP tools. In the second stage, running Claude -p directly revealed the root cause of permission denied errors in non-interactive mode. Due to timing issues, the –allowedTools wildcard was unreliable, so bypassPermissions was used to solve this problem. Verification showed that calendar queries returned correct results.” “✅ Life-Copilot Structured Logging System (structlog + File Rotation + Operation Audit) — Replaced standard library logging with structlog, achieving development-level color output, production-level JSON format, and file rotation of 10MB. audit_log.py was added to three locations: Discord’s on_message, FastAPI middleware, and CLISelector.send, capturing complete conversation records (user input + bot response). The issue of extra fields being lost due to ExtraAdder processor problems was resolved. Four print statements were removed. Currently, there are 420 tests passed.” Implementation and Fixes ✅ Backout of FloatBall Session Changes — More issues arose due to CSS min() modifications and folding processes in some AI sessions. Users requested a backout. The AI mistakenly used git stash to restore all uncommitted changes. After using git stash pop, the specific changes made during this session were manually corrected. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Linux WM fails to handle transparent borderless window sizing atomically, leading to systematic position offset +305px/+113px, flickering detection/compensation mechanisms, and inconsistent sizes between WebView and native windows. Solution: Set the window size to expanded state (152×56), without any adjustments. Use GDK input_shape_combineRegion to control the click area: only the ball area is displayed when folded (56×56), while the entire window is shown when unfolded or during dragging. Key Insight: This is a platform architecture issue, not a computational error. Linux WM interprets the same rect request differently. Solutions need to be found at the sizing level; local fixes can only temporarily solve the problem.
2. Life-Copilot Discord bot continues to report “OAuth authentication required” for calendar queries. There are two overlapping issues at play: (1) Claude -p does not load project-level MCP configurations; (2) permission denied errors occur when using MCP tools in non-interactive mode. Solution: Add –mcp-config and –permission-mode bypassPermissions parameters to the claude_adapter.py command. Running Claude -p directly revealed the root cause of permission denied errors. Since there is a conflict with MCP server initialization, the –allowedTools wildcard is unreliable in non-interactive mode. Using bypassPermissions is the correct method for backend service calls.
3. After implementing the fixed window layout, three platform-specific issues arose: ball display deformation (window builder still uses 56×56 size), inability to expand, and dragging failures (input shape gets blocked after leaving the ball area). Solution: (1) The window builder should use the expanded size and lock min=max based on GTK geometry_hints. (2) CSS justify-content should always take effect based on data-direction. (3) A new set_float_ball_dragging command was added to temporarily expand the input shape during dragging, with it being restored after the process is complete.
Key Insight: WebKitGTK has default minimum size limits. The X11 layer intercepts input shape events before setPointerCapture, so synchronizing the area expansion in Rust is necessary.
General Issues 4. When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, extra {} fields disappear silently from the JSON output. Solution: Add the ExtraAdder() processor to the shared_processors entry, ensuring that additional fields are merged into the structured output.
Key Insight: When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, additional parameters do not get passed automatically. It is necessary to explicitly add these parameters so that the field disappears silently without reporting any errors.
5. When users request a backout of “changes made during this session,” the AI mistakenly uses git stash, which may lead to loss of historical work. Solution: After using git stash pop, manually identify and correct the specific changes made during this session.
Key Insight: Git stash affects all uncommitted changes, so it is not suitable for situations where only “changes made during this session” need to be corrected. It is better to use git diff to clearly define the scope of changes before making individual corrections.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Diagnosing the root cause of Linux FloatBall and selecting solutions | Role | Thought Process | |——|——-| | Human | Completed a comprehensive diagnosis before starting the session: 4 layers of geometric inconsistency, 11 attempts and failures, log evidence (systematic pattern: +305px = expanded width, +113px = contracted width) | | AI | After confirming the diagnosis through the Explore agent, it was organized into six alternative solutions. These solutions were then reviewed and feasibility verified. The fixed window solution was recommended and fully implemented. |
Difference Analysis: Humans far surpass AI in terms of diagnostic depth and evidence quality. The value of AI lies in its ability to build and execute solutions, not just identify problems.
MCP Problem Diagnosis Strategy Role Thought Process Human Continuously forced the AI to conduct deeper investigations instead of accepting superficial explanations. AI Investigated from tokens to MCP server startup to CLI call chains, ultimately using direct tests with Claude -p commands to determine the root cause of permission denied errors. Difference Analysis: Humans ensure that problems are completely resolved, not just hurriedly fixed. AI provides a systematic multi-level investigation method.
Discovery of Unrecorded AI Bot Responses Role Thought Process Human Intuitively asked “How does the Discord bot respond to my messages?” and found that logging only recorded user input while ignoring AI responses. AI Only focused on audit points related to user input during implementation, without considering the integrity of the conversation. Difference Analysis: Humans identified key omissions from the user’s perspective (conversation integrity), while AI only considered system-level audit points.
Requirements Definition for Logging System Role Thought Process Human Simply stated “Write code to be logged,” and after being asked, clearly selected file persistence + structured logging + comprehensive operation audit. AI Scanned existing code and found that 73/114 files already had logging capabilities. Actively identified vague requirements and proposed three directions for the user to choose from. Difference Analysis: Humans posed vague requirements, while AI translated them into specific options through code analysis. The final direction was chosen by humans and executed by AI.
Implementation Level Judgment of Git Backout Scope Role Thought Process Human Intended to back out only the changes made during this AI session, preserving previous work. AI Used git stash operations, which affected a scope beyond what users expected (backed out all uncommitted changes). Difference Analysis: AI failed to accurately understand the difference between “changes made during this session” and “all uncommitted changes,” choosing an overly broad scope of backout.
Priority Selection for FloatBall Functions (Removing Snap Function) Role Thought Process Human After testing and finding that movement was still unstable, explicitly requested the removal of the Snap function, prioritizing core movement issues. AI During the design phase, Snap was listed as a requirement to be retained and implemented. It did not actively propose a phased priority. Difference Analysis: AI tends to implement all functions at once, while users made more pragmatic priorities during testing.
AI Limitations Major Limitations “Unit tests passed, but it’s impossible to predict behavior on specific platforms: All 284 Rust tests and 205 frontend tests passed FloatBall functionality, but issues such as ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures were not caught. Time issues in Claude CLI -p mode—allowing tools to be used in non-interactive mode also failed to pass the test.” “When first implementing, details specific to platforms were often overlooked. User testing or log feedback was needed to identify problems: Window builders had to use expanded sizes, WebKitGTK required GTK geometry_hints constraints, and ExtraAdder should explicitly add when using structlog.” General Limitations “Misjudgment of Git backout scope: When users requested a backout of ‘changes made during this session,’ git stash operations were used, which could lead to loss of historical work.” Achievements Today Core Achievements Linux X11 WM does not guarantee atomicity for transparent borderless windows when sizing. Different WMs interpret the same coordinates differently, leading to systematic offset issues. The only reliable solution is to eliminate sizing functionality (fixed window + GDK input shape). Local patches can only temporarily solve the problem. “Claude CLI -p mode requires two steps to complete MCP configuration: (1) Explicitly pass –mcp-config to load project-level tools; (2) Add –permission-mode bypassPermissions to avoid permission confirmation in non-interactive mode. In this case, the –allowedTools wildcard is unreliable because it conflicts with MCP server initialization. BypassPermissions is the correct method for backend service calls.” GDK Input Shape intercepts events on the X11 layer. setPointerCapture cannot override it. In dragging scenarios, a set_float_ball_dragging command in Rust should be used to temporarily expand the input shape to the entire window. With cairo-rs 0.18 and gdk 0.18, input_shape_combineRegion can be used directly in Tauri with_webview callbacks, eliminating the need for additional complex dependencies. When debugging nested process CLI tools (main process → Claude CLI → MCP server), it’s more efficient to first record the actual command parameters and then isolate and verify the effects of each parameter. The ccplan method effectively transforms complex platform architecture problems into executable plans by exploring alternative solutions, going through a review process, and testing feasibility. The ECL documentation provides cross-session context persistence, reducing the cost of repeated analysis. Practical Achievements When using structlog to wrap standard library logging, it’s necessary to explicitly add extra={} parameters as ExtraAdder() processors so that they appear in the output fields. Otherwise, the field will disappear silently without reporting any errors. Session Summary TokenMonitor 🔄 Linux FloatBall Architecture Reorganization: ccplan Planning and Implementation of Fixed Window Layouts and Multiple Debugments 02:52:43.137 | claude_code
Users provided a comprehensive analysis of the root causes (4 layers of geometric mismatch, 11 failed attempts and reasons, log evidence) to initiate the ccplan process. AI generated six alternative solutions, recommending the fixed window layout with GDK Input Shape. After approval, the implementation was completed. Three issues arose during operation: ball deformation, inability to expand, and dragging failures. These issues were resolved through GTK geometry_hints, CSS direction positioning, and the set_float_ball_dragging command. To ensure stable movement, the snap function was removed. After 284 lines of Rust code and 205 frontend tests, final verification is in progress. During this process, a git stash operation issue was discovered and corrected through stash pop. Then, the specific modifications made during this session were also manually adjusted.
Life-Copilot ✅ Construction of Discord Bot’s Structured Logging System + Complete Fixes to MCP Tool Calls
01:35:00.000 | claude_code
Through ccplan planning, a structured logging system was implemented (development color output/production JSON format, rotated every 10MB). JSONL audit logs were generated at three entry points (Discord/API/CLI) and complete conversation records were saved. After 420 tests, two-stage issues related to MCP tool calls were resolved: First, the –mcp-config parameter was added to load 19 project-specific MCP tools. Then, by running Claude -p directly, the root cause of permission denied errors was found. Using –permission-mode bypassPermissions completely solved this problem in non-interactive mode. Verification showed that in Discord, Google calendar queries returned correct results.
