content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W08 (2026-02-16 ~ 2026-02-22) This week was focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. We completed a systematic investigation of the H&E Image-Only clustering method (established a literature baseline for ARI between 0.11–0.16), implemented three self-supervised enhancement schemes (SCAN improved ARI from 0.251 to 0.303, +20.6%), and established a two-stage fusion framework for Vision Refinement. Meanwhile, we parallelly carried out the Error Recovery Benchmark on the tianhe cluster (M14 evaluation of infrastructure verification, full-scenario evaluation started at 649 scenarios), and built up the data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5 (18.4GB MimicGen dataset stored, training configuration ready). Throughout the week, we resolved several engineering issues such as STEGO NaN, double normalization bug, lerobot version conflict, and HuggingFace proxy issues. The OOM issues of Pi0.5 and video verification for visualize_scene.py were postponed to next week.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-16 ~ 2026-02-22 Active Days 3 / 7 Total Conversations 9 Projects Involved 4 Completed Tasks 11 Ongoing Tasks 5 Total Tokens 28,509,501 Total Cost $14.13 Average Cost per Day $4.71 Project Progress MIHD (Spatial Transcriptomics Clustering) (3 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Completed four core technical documents: RM-IDEAL bilingual instructions, guidelines for using visual encoders, literature review of pathological PFM, evaluation analysis of UNI/UNI2 original papers Conducted a systematic investigation of the H&E Image-Only method, established a literature baseline for DLPFC ARI (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11), and analyzed five root causes of Foundation Model failure Implemented and verified three self-supervised enhancement schemes: STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN. SCAN achieved the best result with ARI=0.303 (baseline 0.251, +20.6%). We also verified the complementary nature of its embedding with gene features (mean fusion +0.065 ARI) Identified and fixed a double normalization bug in eval_scan_fusion.py for STAIG. Decided to use the correct path in run_benchmark.py instead of fixing the independent script Integrated CacheManager embedding caching in run_benchmark.py (loaded in seconds, supports custom cache names for scan_uni2) Implemented a two-stage fusion framework for Vision Refinement (~60 lines of minimal intrusion). Started batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies in the background Re-generated all 11 sections visualizations in batches (using .npz caching, no need for re-inference), and added an H&E original image panel Blocked Items:
⚠️ The coordinate dimension bug in eval_scan_fusion.py caused incomplete debugging of the SCAN fusion evaluation script ⚠️ The first experiment with Vision Refinement (scan_cluster + concat, ARI=0.313) was lower than direct concat (0.387). The issue of diversity of self-supervised compression loss features needs further analysis ⚠️ The implementation of ImageEncoder for the big goal 7 of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md has not yet started Error Recovery Benchmark & Phoenix pi0.5 Replica (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Developed a complete Phase II execution plan covering 7 steps, covering G1–G7 big goals and M12–M15 milestones. The critical path is about 16 days M14 baseline evaluation of infrastructure verification (10 scenarios passed sanity check), full-scenario CPU evaluation started at 649 scenarios (surpassing expectations by 454, +43%) Completed the full data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5: convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py, evaluate_mimicgen.py, OpenPI training configuration (100K steps, 4-GPUs), and downloaded 18.4GB MimicGen dataset with format conversion completed in tasks 7-8/9 Diagnosed the OOM bottleneck of Pi0.5, standardized GPU access from SSH to srun –overlap Resolved compatibility issues between lerobot 0.1.0 and datasets>=4.0 (downgraded to 3.6.0) Established hf-mirror.com as the standard data acquisition solution for the cluster’s HuggingFace Blocked Items:
⚠️ The OOM issue of Pi0.5 remains unresolved (GPU memory insufficient by 150MB), and baseline evaluation is still blocked ⚠️ The video verification of visualize_scene.py is blocked due to SLURM node permissions issues ⚠️ The Pi0 VLA Server port conflict (port 5555 occupied) caused session interruptions Key Tasks ✅ Systematic investigation of H&E Image-Only clustering method (2026-02-19) — Collected information on methods such as MILWRM/F-SEG/Deep Contrastive Clustering. Verified the value of DLPFC ARI in image-only experiments (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11). Conducted in-depth research on the application of BYOL/STEGO/SCAN in pathology. Reviewed frameworks for addressing domain gaps in the CV community ✅ Established MIHD technical documentation system (2026-02-19) — Created four core technical documents: RM-IDEAL bilingual structure document, guidelines for using visual encoders (12 chapters), literature review of pathological PFM, evaluation analysis of UNI/UNI2 original papers (34 clinical tasks) ✅ Analyzed root causes of Foundation Model failure in spatial domain recognition (2026-02-19) — Conducted a systematic analysis from five dimensions: training data domain mismatch, pre-training task mismatch, minimal morphological differences between brain tissue layers, feature redundancy, and lack of spatial context. Provided case studies using the UNI2 brown repetitive patch phenomenon ✅ Implemented Image-Only clustering enhancement schemes (STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN) (2026-02-19) — Created four model files: STEGOHead/BYOLAdapter/SpatialGATRefiner/SCANHead. Conducted comparative tests on 151673 sections, with SCAN achieving the best result with ARI=0.303 (+20.6%) ✅ Investigated double normalization bug in STAIG fusion and made architectural decisions (2026-02-20) — Confirmed that not passing staig_alignment_config in eval_scan_fusion.py caused StandardScaler to be used again within STAIGTrainer. Decided to use run_benchmark.py instead of fixing the independent script ✅ Integrated embedding caching mechanism in run_benchmark.py (2026-02-20) — Introduced CacheManager, loading cache before gene/vision encoding and skipping encoder instantiation if hit. Supported custom cache names for scan_uni2, with vision caches available in standard/freq/staig_strict variants 🔄 M14 baseline evaluation of infrastructure verification and full-scenario evaluation started (2026-02-22) — sanity check (60 episodes, about 7 minutes per episode, SR=0% as expected). Subsequently, started full CPU evaluation with 649 scenarios × 2 strategies × 3 seeds (about 3894 episodes), running in the background 🔄 Established the complete data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5 (2026-02-22) — Written convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py and evaluate_mimicgen.py, configured pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training parameters. Downloaded 18.4GB MimicGen dataset through hf-mirror.com (9 tasks with 9000 demos), completed format conversion in tasks 7-8/9 🔄 Joint evaluation of SCAN embedding and multi-modal fusion (2026-02-19) — Written eval_scan_fusion.py to compare the optimized 256-dimensional visual embedding with PCA gene features across all fusion methods. Mean fusion achieved ARI +0.065, partially fixed the coordinates dimension bug, script debugging in progress 🔄 Implemented two-stage fusion framework for MIHD Vision Refinement and batch experiments (2026-02-22) — Added –vision_refine parameter (scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial), with minimal intrusion of about 60 lines of code. The first experiment achieved ARI=0.313, which was lower than baseline concat 0.387. Batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies are running in the background ✅ Formulated complete execution plan for Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II (2026-02-22) — Analyzed dependencies between G1–G7 big goals and M12–M15 milestones, developed an execution plan covering 7 steps. Clarified GPU allocation strategy (srun –overlap with at least 50% free memory), with a critical path of about 16 days ✅ Diagnosed Error Recovery Benchmark baseline issues and updated GPU access specifications (2026-02-22) — Confirmed that Pi0.5 OOM (150MB insufficient GPU memory) and BC-RNN obs key issues have been resolved, and 649 scenarios are ready. Standardized GPU access methods in CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md to srun –overlap 🚫 Force parameter expansion for visualize_scene.py (2026-02-22) — Completed adding force_override/duration_override/settle_steps parameters and Phase 3 neutral action logic. Video verification is still blocked due to SLURM node permissions issues ✅ Special comparison experiment with STAIG fusion (151673) (2026-02-20) — Tested staig_fusion × {UNI2_raw, SCAN(UNI2)} separately. ARI values were 0.3929/0.3880 respectively, almost equal. Verified that StandardScaler + PCA within STAIG offset the optimization effect of SCAN ✅ Added H&E panel to UNI2 visualization and updated in batches (2026-02-19) — Modified to a 1×3 layout (H&E + GT + prediction). Re-generated all 11 sections visualizations using .npz caching, with 151510 successfully repaired through hires→lowres symlinks Issues and Solutions 1. STEGO training loss was NaN throughout the process. A dense similarity matrix of 3639×3639 caused overflow when temperature=0.07 [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Two-step fix: Normalize the input image_emb; replace InfoNCE with a numerically stable version (reduce maximum value before taking logsumexp, increase temperature to 0.1)
2. MILWRM was incorrectly classified as an Image-Only method. The first AI summary mixed multi-modal methods with image-only results. The conclusions of the first research need significant revision [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Use WebFetch to read the full PMC text to confirm that MILWRM actually relies on gene expression. Specifically extract image-only data points from ablation experiments in articles like SpaConTDS
3. There was a double normalization bug in staig_fusion in eval_scan_fusion.py (StandardScaler used again within STAIGTrainer) [MIHD] (2026-02-20) Solution: Abandon fixing the independent script and directly use run_benchmark.py, which correctly passes staig_alignment_config. Reuse the verified path
4. In run_benchmark.py, the encoder was re-instantiated to extract embeddings every time, lacking pipeline-level caching support [MIHD] (2026-02-20) Solution: Integrate CacheManager from pipeline/cache_manager.py. Load cache before encoding and write it back after extraction. Skip encoder instantiation if hit
5. Lerobot 0.1.0 was incompatible with datasets>=4.0 (torch.stack reported TypeError: Column object replaced list) [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Downgrade datasets from 4.4.1 to 3.6.0; for datasets 4.0, change the return value of dataset[‘column’] from list to Column object. Lerobot expects a list/tuple of tensors
6. HuggingFace official download failed due to proxy (Squid 503), and Python download script couldn’t connect [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Use hf-mirror.com + wget instead. URL format: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过集群 HTTP proxy available at 40–200MB/s
7. Unstable access to Slurm GPU nodes: srun without –overlap caused commands to hang, direct SSH connection was unreliable, multi-partition submissions were rejected [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Standardize the workflow: source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid= –overlap; prohibit direct SSH to GPU nodes
8. In eval_scan_fusion.py, there was an anomaly in spatial coordinate dimensions (became (1,2)), causing errors for multiple fusion methods [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Abandon calling load_spatial_coordinates() (barcode matching failed). Instead, directly read coordinates from adata.obsm[‘spatial’]. Fix the error in unpacking return values of load_dlpfc_data
Learnings Domain Knowledge### Architecture SCAN is the most effective method for image-only spatial transcriptomics tasks, with an ARI of 0.303 and a gain of +20.6%. Its core advantage lies in offline feature k-NN mining, which decouples embedding learning from clustering. The 256-dimensional optimized embeddings, when fused with gene features, show strong complementarity (mean fusion +0.065 ARI). Two-stage fusion is not necessarily superior to one-stage fusion: After compressing the visual embedding from 1536d to 256d using scan_cluster, the ARI of multi-modal fusion (0.313) is lower than that of direct concatenation (0.387). This indicates that self-supervised compression may lose the original feature diversity required for fusion tasks. The goals of self-supervised clustering optimization and downstream fusion tasks are inconsistent. STAIG fusion uses StandardScaler+PCA preprocessing to absorb the effects of external embedding optimization (the difference in ARI between SCAN and UNI2_raw is only 0.005). Embeddings should be saved as raw embeddings before normalization, and post-encoder normalization should be performed after loading to ensure consistent results across different call paths. VLA baseline evaluations must clearly state the source of checkpoints: Models fine-tuned on non-target datasets (pi0_libero, pi05_base) are evaluated for zero-shot cross-domain recovery capabilities. The paper must explicitly state this experimental setup, and additional comparative experiments after fine-tuning are needed to fully demonstrate the usefulness of the dataset. In complex experimental systems, it is advisable to reuse existing, validated tool paths (such as run_benchmark.py). Independent scripts can easily introduce hidden bugs due to inconsistent preprocessing. Before calling internal functions within the project, the source code should be read in real-time to verify function signatures, rather than relying on memory. Debugging When calculating large-scale dense similarity matrices (n>3000) using InfoNCE with comparative loss, it is necessary to use numerically stable log-sum-exp (minus the row maximum). Otherwise, exponential operations with temperature=0.07 under float32 precision may overflow and cause NaN values. This is a key engineering constraint for implementing large-scale comparative learning. Tools Lerobot 0.1.0 has strict constraints on dataset versions; it should be pinned to <4.0 (recommended 3.x). Dataset 4.0 changes the return value of dataset[‘column’] to a Column object, causing a TypeError in torch.stack. MimicGen is fully compatible with LIBERO obs/action format (84×84 images, 8D states, 7D actions), and can directly reuse OpenPI’s LeRobotLiberoDataConfig. The standard way to access HuggingFace from Chinese mainland HPC clusters is via hf-mirror.com. The URL format is: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过 wget + HTTP proxy with a speed of 40–200MB/s. This should be the default approach. OpenPI’s JAX training natively supports multi-GPU data parallelism. Just specify the list of GPUs using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, and JAX will automatically build a 2D mesh parallel without modification to TrainConfig. Slurm –overlap is a key parameter for running new commands on existing interactive jobs, and it is a core technique for calling cluster GPU nodes in the Claude Code environment. Notes on AI Usage Effective Patterns: ✓ Parallel sub-tasks are used to explore project structures and develop cleanup plans (MIHD organizes tasks), reducing the time spent on serial exploration. ✓ Three methods (STEGO/BYOL/SCAN) are executed in parallel on GPUs to compare and verify, significantly reducing the total experiment time. ✓ Using .npz caches allows for batch re-generation of visualizations, completely decoupling inference from visualization. This worked well with 11/11 sections. ✓ Independent temporary scripts (_test_staig_scan.py) isolate tests for individual fusion methods, reducing the task duration from 2 hours to 30 seconds. ✓ Independent exploration of JAX’s multi-GPU mechanism (no need to modify config) and alternative download solutions via hf-mirror.com are independent tasks that do not require user intervention. ✓ The minimum invasive code modification (about 60 lines) is made in run_benchmark.py by inserting the Vision Refinement stage, maintaining the stability of the architecture. Limitations: ✗ Inaccurate classification of literature: MILWRM was incorrectly classified as an image-only method. Initial research results were mixed with multi-modal results, requiring two interventions from users to converge to the correct research boundary. ✗ Lack of awareness to actively expose key experimental assumptions: The paper did not explicitly state how VLA evaluation using LIBERO fine-tuned checkpoints affects the validity of the experiment. This topic was only discussed after user inquiries. ✗ API usage tends to rely on memory rather than real-time verification: eval_scan_fusion.py had multiple errors in function signatures/return values. The source code should be read in before calling. ✗ Slurm permission issues led to multiple attempts to access partitions (5+ times). The correct account/partition information was not quickly obtained from users. ✗ Insufficient status monitoring of background tasks: Data conversion was still ongoing when LeRobot dataset verification was triggered, resulting in false timestamp violation errors. Frequent sleep polling was interrupted multiple times. ✗ In the HPC environment, CPU is used for model validation by default, hiding real performance issues and causing additional interaction problems. Next Week’s Plans Next week will focus on three parallel tasks: ①MIHD: Fixing bugs in eval_scan_fusion.py to evaluate SCAN embeddings and full fusion strategies. Analyze batch experiment results of 7 fusion strategies and diagnose the root causes of Vision Refinement compression loss features. Consider adjusting refinement hidden_dim or switching between stego_refine/byol_spatial methods. ②Error Recovery Benchmark: Organize results of the full CPU evaluation for M14 649 scenarios to solve Pi0.5 OOM issues (request for larger memory GPUs or reduce batch size). Start training Phoenix pi0.5 with 100K steps on 4-GPUs. ③Engineering Block Elimination: Resolve SLURM node permission issues to complete video verification of visualize_scene.py. Address Pi0 VLA server port conflicts (detect using lsof -i:5555 + alternate port mechanism) and advance the implementation of ImageEncoder enhancement in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md.

summary: This week, we focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. We completed a systematic investigation into H&E Image-Only clustering (establishing a literature baseline for ARI values ranging from 0.11 to 0.16), implemented three self-supervised enhancement methods (SCAN improved ARI from 0.251 to 0.303, a +20.6% increase), and established a two-stage framework for Vision Refinement. Meanwhile, we continued to work on Error Recovery Benchmarking on the tianhe cluster (M14 evaluation of infrastructure, initialization of full-scenario evaluations with 649 scenarios). We also prepared the Phoenix pi0.5 data pipeline (storage of 18.4GB MimicGen dataset, training configuration ready). Throughout the week, we resolved several engineering issues, including STEGO NaN, dual normalization bugs, version conflicts with lerobot, and problems with HuggingFace proxies. The remaining issues related to Pi0.5 OOM and visualizing scenes via video verification will be addressed next week.

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W07 (2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15) This week, we focused on three areas: robot simulation, bioinformatics, and toolchain development. In the error_recovery_benchmark project, deep bottlenecks were encountered during force injection debugging. A 30N force applied to the OSC controller had no noticeable effect, indicating a fundamental issue with the controller’s countermeasure mechanism. For MIHD, the Chinese documentation for the enhancement plan was completed. ccusage implemented support for GLM multi-model billing and passed type/format checks. Robobrain_pi confirmed that the training process is ready. This week, AI showed obvious pattern-fitting defects in repeated errors (SSH lacking the cd command) and environmental assumptions (proxy, pnpm). Human intervention provided more efficient debugging ideas at critical decision points (extreme force testing, local GPU detection, reuse of existing pricing files).
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15 Active Days 4 / 7 Total Conversations 10 Projects Involved 6 Completed Tasks 10 Ongoing Tasks 2 Total Tokens 54,947,656 Total Cost $23.28 Claude Code Tokens 40,980,880 Claude Code Costs $19.02 Codex Tokens 13,966,776 Codex Costs $4.26 Average Daily Cost $5.82 Project Progress error_recovery_benchmark (2 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Verified the current state of approximately 6200 lines of code in the framework, identified high-priority bottlenecks (collision geometry name mapping, target object dynamic recognition) Created AGENTS.md contributor guide (372 words, covering structure/construction/testing/submission guidelines) Implemented –force_override command-line parameter and enhanced force injection mechanism (force range extended to [15,45]N, clip set to 60N, duration automatically cleared after completion) Generated a 30N force injection test video (451KB, 193 frames), establishing a visual force debugging toolchain Bottlenecks:
⚠️ No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N force: The OSC controller (kp=150) can cancel out the impulse within duration_steps=1 (about 20ms). Whether the force injection mechanism works at all remains unverified ⚠️ Complete E2E verification requires GPU node (an53), currently mitigated through hierarchical testing (CPU unit/smoke tests) MIHD (2 active days) — ✅ completed Completed:
Translated ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md into a structured Chinese version (6 major goals: normalization, UNI2+scGPT, Q-Former/LLaVA, niche query, batch correction, full configuration) Written the translated content in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md, preserving key details like file names, CLI parameters, and configuration fields ccusage (GLM billing support) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Implemented loadLocalPricing() in _pricing-fetcher.ts to load LiteLLM format pricing from CCUSAGE_PRICING_FILE environment variable or default path ~/.ccusage/ccusage.json, and merged it into the offline cache Fixed three issues: misuse of Result.try() API, filtering of LiteLLM metadata keys, and global variables in ESLint process All format/typechecks and targeted tests passed Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Global testing showed failures unrelated to this change for apps/amp. This needs confirmation before merging the PR robobrain_pi (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Confirmed datasets/demo_v2.hdf5 exists with 50 trajectories and 7-dimensional actions Verified that the complete SAC framework is in place (sac_agent.py, trainer.py, train_sac.py) Confirmed availability of 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Recommended use of GPU 1-3 for training. Provided training command instructions Reconstructed the full project timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 from .ccusage/summaries/ gadget/research documentation (1 active day) — ⏸️ paused Completed:
Initiated updates to research/CLAUDE.md. Users completed preliminary reading of the research directory structure Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Conversations were interrupted after users issued analysis requests. AI has not actually performed or written any analysis yet Key Tasks 🚫 Root cause of ineffective force injection with 30N (2026-02-14) — No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N to the Sawyer arm. Users proposed verifying the force injection mechanism using infinite force. Conversations were interrupted before implementation 🔄 ccusage GLM model billing support (2026-02-12) — Added logic for loading and merging local LiteLLM format pricing files in _pricing-fetcher.ts. Targeted tests passed, but global testing showed unrelated failures that need confirmation ✅ Prepare robobrain_pi SAC Pick-and-Place training environment (2026-02-10) — Confirmed availability of datasets, SAC framework, and 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Provided training command instructions ✅ Implement –force_override parameter and enhanced force injection (2026-02-14) — Added command-line parameters, expanded force range to [15,45]N, implemented automatic clearing after duration, fixed bug in ImpulseInjector nested configuration path ✅ Check progress of error_recovery_benchmark plan (2026-02-10) — Confirmed high-priority bottlenecks: collision geometry name mapping (collision.py/env_wrapper.py) and target object dynamic recognition. Complete E2E verification requires GPU node 🔄 Update gadget research/CLAUDE.md documentation (2026-02-10) — Users requested in-depth reading of the research directory before updating the design document. Conversations were interrupted. AI has not actually performed analysis yet ✅ Generate and verify 30N force injection visualization video (2026-02-14) — Ran 2_visualize_scene.py –force_override 30 to generate impulse_demo_0_step72.mp4 (451KB, 193 frames). No visible disturbance was observed visually ✅ Restore robobrain_pi project history from ccusage summary (2026-02-10) — Read .ccusage/summaries/ for 10 Markdown files to reconstruct the full timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 Issues and Solutions 1. Complete E2E verification of error_recovery_benchmark is hindered by GPU node dependencies (EGL/robosuite runtime constraints) [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-10) Solution: Define a clear testing strategy: perform CPU unit tests and smoke tests first. Delay complete E2E verification until the GPU node (an53) is available to avoid blocking development
2. @praha/byethrow’s Result.try() API was misused: AI used it directly as an result, while it actually returns a reusable parser function [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: Store the return value of Result.try({…}) as a function, and explicitly call this function to obtain Result<T, E>
3. The LiteLLM pricing JSON contained metadata keys like sample_spec, which were treated as empty pricing entries after parsing with valibot schema, leading to test assertion failures [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: After schema parsing, additionally check that input_cost_per_token or output_cost_per_token is not null. Filter out metadata entries without pricing data
4. Multiple attempts to SSH to the GPU node were rejected by pam_slurm_adopt (active SLURM job required), resulting in significant wasted time [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-14) Solution: Users suggested checking whether the current node has a GPU using nvidia-smi -L. It was found that there were already 5 A800 GPUs on the current node, so no SSH transfer was required
Learnings Architecture Using external summary tools (ccusage) to export historical sessions is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context limitations: externalizing session summaries into files allows AI to quickly restore project context in new sessions, avoiding repeated background description Testing strategies for large robot RL projects should be clearly hierarchical: CPU unit tests, CPU smoke tests, GPU E2E testing. Avoid blocking the entire test process due to GPU unavailability Domain Knowledge The OSC controller (kp=150, Λ≈5-10kg) has strong resistance to external forces. An impulse with duration_steps=1 (about 20ms) is canceled out within the controller’s response cycle, effectively equivalent to no force application. To make disturbances visible, continuous force application must be maintained for several control cycles or use forces far beyond what the controller can handle MIHD enhancement plan is implemented in a strict dependency order: configuration basics (Idea 6 Phase 1) → normalization → UNI2+scGPT experiments → Q-Former/LLaVA → niche query → batch correction → full configuration integration. This order is designed to avoid cumulative refactoring costs Tools @praha/byethrow’s Result.try({try, catch}) returns a factory function (which must be called explicitly), rather than directly executing and returning a result. This differs from common Rust/fp-ts implementations of the Result monad. Special attention is needed In HPC clusters, use nvidia-smi -L to check whether the current node has a GPU before executing GPU tasks. If you need to SSH to remote nodes, you must explicitly change directory to the project folder and activate the conda environment in the remote command (SSH does not inherit the current shell working directory) ccusage’s offline pricing is pre-filtered using the isClaudeModel function in _macro.ts during construction. Extending multi-model support requires simultaneous modifications to both macro and _pricing-fetcher.ts ESLint rules for this project require all Node.js built-in objects to be explicitly imported via the node: protocol (e.g., import process from ’node:process’). Global objects cannot be used directly Debugging When debugging force injection or other physical mechanisms, it is important to first verify whether the mechanism itself works using extreme parameters (such as infinite force, 1000 consecutive steps), and then gradually adjust the parameters to a reasonable range. This should not be done starting from reasonable parameters AI Usage Notes Effective Solutions:
✓ Reconstructing cross-session project history by reading Markdown files in the ccusage summaries/ directory is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context window limitations ✓ Static code analysis (reading Makefile, test code, existing documentation) can effectively generate contributor guidelines when there is no git history ✓ Binary extreme testing ideas (first verifying the mechanism with infinite force, then adjusting parameters to a reasonable range) are more efficient than systematic code analysis when debugging physical simulations Limitations:
✗ Repeating the same error (SSH lacking the cd command) 15+ times within a single session. There is no ability to correct behavior based on repeated failures ✗ There are incorrect assumptions about library API behavior (Result.try() factory pattern). These can only be corrected after testing fails ✗ Failed to identify existing data assets (local ccusage.json pricing files). The default approach is to modify code logic rather than reuse existing files ✗ Mechanically following historical command templates (proxy settings) without verifying their applicability. Human intervention is needed to indicate that the task does not require network access Next Week’s Plans Next week will focus on three areas: (1) Debugging the root cause of force injection in error_recovery_benchmark — first verify whether the xfrc_applied mechanism works at all using extreme forces (infinite or 1000 consecutive steps), then investigate body mounting points and force direction issues. Ultimately, solve the collision geometry name mapping, which is a key E2E bottleneck; (2) ccusage GLM billing support — confirm that unrelated tests for apps/amp do not affect merging progress and move forward with the PR; (3) Update gadget/research CLAUDE.md — complete the interrupted documentation analysis and writing tasks. Robobrain.pi SAC training can be started in parallel when the GPU node is available, and we will observe the convergence curve.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-09

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W06 (2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08) This week (2026-02-06~07), the work was focused on two main areas: first, systematically diagnosing the ARI performance gap between staig_fusion in the MIHD project and the original STAIG (0.21→target 0.56). Five key implementation differences were identified and quantified. Additionally, we completed the code layer work for strict semantic alignment. Second, we engineered improvements to the benchmark tool, fixed the bug in rendering bar charts, and implemented an end-to-end GitHub Pages automatic publishing workflow along with CLI interaction for uploading files. Furthermore, I practiced English speaking for TOEFL (test of oral proficiency).
Week Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08 Active Days 1 / 7 Total Conversations 2 Projects Involved 2 Completed Tasks 7 Ongoing Tasks 2 Total Tokens 88,552,896 Total Cost $28.25 Claude Code Tokens 4,682,683 Claude Code Costs $1.00 Codex Tokens 83,870,213 Codex Costs $27.25 Average Daily Cost $14.12 Project Progress MIHD / STAIG Fusion (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Compared the original STAIG notebook with MIHD implementation, identified five quantifiable key differences and ranked them by priority. Identified the root cause: coordinates of full-resolution images (13332×13332) still used a scaling factor of 0.15, and patch points deviated significantly from the tissue area. Completed code modifications for strict semantic alignment in staig_fusion (4 core files), implementing default mclust, HVG original features, STAIG hyperparameters, and spatial majority vote refinement. Fixed the issue where mclust silently downgraded to kmeans when unavailable, added pre-validation for embedding shape. Identified issues with reverse gene preprocessing order (MIHD first HVG then normalize/log/scale; opposite in original STAIG). Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Mixing coordinate scales is the top priority issue. Verification during runtime is still needed after modifications. ⚠️ The root cause of mclust dimension being zero (which step generated an empty embedding) needs to be confirmed after next run. ⚠️ HPC environment did not fully install rpy2 and R mclust packages, so the risk of silent downgrading remains. Benchmark Tool (gadget) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Fixed the bug in rendering short bars for high scores using Plotly JS CDN version, forced list type, and set rangemode=‘tozero’. Implemented an end-to-end GitHub Pages publishing workflow: verification/duplication/masking → repository_dispatch → queue files → daily batch processing → automatic deployment. Added scripts/ingest_submissions.py (verification/duplication/cleaning) and scripts/submit_result.py. Implemented three GitHub Actions workflows: accept-submission, daily-publish, pages-deploy. Added interactive upload prompts to the benchmark CLI, supporting –upload/-no-upload and environment variable configuration. Removed the Historical Trends block and fixed an AttributeError. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ The issue of low GPU utilization (both CPU and GPU were low, with single-threaded I/O waiting as a bottleneck) has not been fully resolved. Users need to choose an optimization plan. English Learning (TOEFL) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Completed practice for Question 4 in TOEFL speaking (Bystander Effect), achieving a score of 4.5/5. Practiced 4 questions this week, improving from 3.5 to 4.5. The main weakness was grammar errors related to subject-verb agreement. Key Tasks ✅ Diagnosing the ARI/NMI performance gap between STAIG fusion and original STAIG (2026-02-06) — Compared two code paths, identified and quantified five root causes: ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC is unavailable; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference of 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformation differences. ✅ Implementing GitHub Pages automatic publishing workflow (2026-02-06) — Added a new relay architecture, three GitHub Actions workflows to achieve end-to-end data collection, duplication, cleaning, and automatic publishing. 🔄 Implementing strict STAIG semantic alignment in staig_fusion (2026-02-06) — Modified 4 core files to achieve strict STAIG semantic alignment. Syntax verification passed. Verification of mclust dimension errors is still needed during runtime. ✅ Fixing the bar chart rendering bug in benchmark reports (2026-02-06) — Locked Plotly JS CDN version to 3.3.1, changed Series to list format, set rangemode=‘tozero’ to completely fix short bars issue for high scores. 🔄 Fixing the mclust dimension is zero runtime error (2026-02-06) — Added pre-validation for embedding shape at the Python level (2D, non-zero rows/columns, sample count >= cluster count). The root cause will be confirmed after next run. ✅ Adding interactive upload prompts to the benchmark CLI (2026-02-06) — Asked users if they wanted to upload after running, supporting –upload/-no-upload and environment variables BENCHMARK_RELAY_URL. Problems and Solutions 1. MIHD staig_fusion’s ARI (0.21/0.4849) is much lower than the original STAIG’s (0.562), root cause unknown [MIHD / STAIG Fusion] (2026-02-06) Solution: Compared two code paths, identified five root causes and ranked them by priority: ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC is unavailable; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference of 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters not fully aligned.
2. Plotly bar chart rendering error: Short bars for high scores, visual logic reversed [Benchmark Tool] (2026-02-06) Solution: The root cause was that Plotly 6.x used binary-encoded arrays serialization, which was incompatible with older CDN versions. Solution: Locked the CDN version to match the Python package version (3.3.1), forced a Python list input, and set rangemode=‘tozero’.
3. Strict STAIG mode forces visual encoder to UNI, users think it’s using UNI2, behavior unclear [MIHD / STAIG Fusion] (2026-02-06) Solution: Added clear hints about encoder override in logs; the UNI2 progress bar was not displayed because UNI was actually used.
4. create_trend_chart AttributeError: Method is commented out but still called in generate_html [Benchmark Tool] (2026-02-06) Solution: Added a stub method that returns an empty chart and completely removed the calling code.
Learning Outcomes Architecture The correct layered architecture for GitHub Pages submissions: Public users → relay (verification/throttling/masking) → repository_dispatch → queue files → daily batch processing (duplication/cleaning/appending CSV) → commit/push → automatic pages deployment. The relay layer is a key isolation layer to protect write permissions of the repository. Interface names must strictly match actual behavior: The name ‘staig_fusion’ naturally implies the same semantics as STAIG. Allowing significant differences in default behavior can lead to many hidden errors. Global override behaviors (such as forcing UNI as the encoder) must be clearly indicated in logs. Debugging Plotly 6.x uses binary-encoded arrays serialization by default, which causes bar length parsing errors when it’s incompatible with older CDN versions. Solution: Strictly lock the CDN version to match the Python package version, forcing a Python list input instead of pandas Series. Low CPU and GPU utilization indicates that the program is waiting in single-threaded I/O, not just CPU. Visual feature extraction performance bottlenecks usually occur in data preprocessing (patch extraction) rather than model forward passes. When R interfaces (like mclust) fail in Python, the error message isn’t intuitive. Sufficient pre-validation should be added at the Python level, and clear error messages with shape information should be provided. If an R package is unavailable, a clear warning must be given for silent degradation. The algorithm behavior shouldn’t be changed without any hints. Domain Knowledge There are five quantifiable key differences between MIHD and original STAIG (ranked by priority): ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformation differences. The ARI gap (0.21→0.56) is mainly determined by points ① and ②. Notes on AI Usage Effective Modes:
✓ Systematic cross-repository code comparison: By comparing two code libraries line by line, we quantified and identified multiple implementation differences and ranked them by priority. ✓ Multi-turn tool invocation combined with human observation: AI detected coordinate scale issues (technical details), while users noticed encoder override problems (runtime observations), creating a complementary approach. ✓ Architecture design supplementation: Humans proposed core security intuitions (confirmation before upload, secure isolation), while AI translated them into complete executable technical architectures. Limitations:
✗ Insufficient fix-ups: When removing trend charts, only the calling function was removed initially, and additional fixes were needed. ✗ Easy to overlook existing global override logic: Didn’t realize that strict mode would force visual encoder override when adding UNI2 tqdm. ✗ Didn’t check HPC environment availability of modules (like scanpy) in advance, resulting in a ModuleNotFoundError during verification. This increased the number of debugging rounds. ✗ Provided multiple options for low GPU utilization issues, but didn’t actively recommend using profile tools (py-spy, nvprof) to accurately identify the actual bottleneck. Next Week’s Plan The highest priority next week is to verify the runtime effects of strict STAIG alignment modifications: ① Fixing the issue with mixing coordinate scales (full-resolution images should no longer use scaling factors), which is the most critical step for improving ARI; ② Confirm the root cause of mclust dimension being zero and fix it; ③ Install rpy2 and R mclust packages to eliminate the risk of silent degradation in the HPC environment. For the benchmark tool, we can selectively promote GPU utilization optimization (multi-process DataLoader or patch cache pre-extraction schemes). In terms of English learning, we will continue to practice for grammar weaknesses like subject-verb agreement.

content:
Today’s problem 3432. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference
Intuition In this question, we need to partition the array into two parts. And the difference between these two parts are even.
Now, both parts must have the same module to 2. That is, they are both even or both odd. So, the sum of the array needs to be even.
Now, if the sum of the array is even, then every partition must have the same module to 2. Otherwise, the sum of the array would be odd.
Therefore, the answer would be 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Approach Return 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Space complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Code class Solution: def countPartitions(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: if sum(nums) % 2 == 1: return 0 return len(nums) - 1
summary: 1. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference

content:
Today’s problem 3068. Find the Maximum Sum of Node Values
Important: all the methods below are based on this fact: xor even times equals xor zero times. Method 1: Tree DP Intuition and Approach In this problem, if we only consider one direction, e.g., from root to leaf, then the process will not have after effect (later decisions will not affect previous ones). Therefore, we can use DP to solve this problem.
The hardest part is the definition of the dp. As we have a prerequisite of a direction, a better way to define the dp formula is to exclude the effect of current node. Also, for each node, there are two status, as described above, each node can either xor odd times or even times.
Therefore, we have our DP definition. $dp[x][0/1]$ means the largest value the children of x can achieve when the node x is changed (1) or unchanged (0).
Now, for each child c of node x, we can do two operations: either do xor for both node x and c, or do not do xor for neither x nor c.
The dp formula of these two operations will be: (Note: the priority of $\oplus$ is lower than $+$, so it is very important to add a parentheses.)
Do the xor operation $dp[x][0] = max(dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][0] + dp[c][1] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. $dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][1] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][1] + dp[c][1] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. NOT do the xor operation $dp[x][0] = max(dp[x][1] + dp[c][1] + nums[c], dp[x][1] + dp[c][0] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. $dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. Note that the dp[x][0] and dp[x][1] should be renewed at the same time.
Moreover, another important thing is the initialization of the dp array. For all $dp[x][1]$, we will give it a value of $-inf$, so that we can avoid the case when c is a leaf node and the number is $\oplus$ with k contributes to the $dp[x]$ array.
The final result will be $max((dp[0][0] + nums[0]), (dp[0][1] + (nums[0] ^ k)))$
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp = [[0 for _ in range(2)] for _ in range(n)] for i in range(n): dp[i][1] = -10_000_000_000 edge = [[] for _ in range(n)] for x,y in edges: edge[x].append(y) edge[y].append(x) def dfs(x, fa): for to in edge[x]: if to == fa: continue dfs(to, x) c0 = max(dp[to][0] + nums[to], dp[to][1] + (nums[to] ^ k)) c1 = max(dp[to][0] + (nums[to] ^ k), dp[to][1] + nums[to]) dp[x][0], dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][0] + c0, dp[x][1] + c1), max(dp[x][1] + c0, dp[x][0] + c1) dfs(0,-1) return max((dp[0][0] + nums[0]), (dp[0][1] + (nums[0] ^ k))) Method 2: Tree DP with better memory Intuition and Approach In the previous code, we find that the $dp[x]$ will only use two times. Once in calculating the result of $dp[x]$, once in calculating the result of $dp[fa]$.
Therefore, we can return the value of $dp[x][0]$ and $dp[x][1]$ to avoid the extra space of the dp array.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) edge = [[] for _ in range(n)] for x,y in edges: edge[x].append(y) edge[y].append(x) def dfs(x, fa): dp0,dp1 = 0,-1e9 for to in edge[x]: if to == fa: continue c0, c1 = dfs(to, x) dp0, dp1 = max(dp0 + c0, dp1 + c1), max(dp0 + c1, dp1 + c0) return max(dp0 + nums[x], dp1 + (nums[x] ^ k)), max(dp0 + (nums[x] ^ k), dp1 + nums[x]) return dfs(0,-1)[0] Important: all the methods below are based on this fact: there are always a path between two nodes on a tree. Therefore, we can $\oplus$ all the nodes on this path, resulting the $\oplus$ of any two nodes on the tree. Method 3: DP without tree Intuition and Approach For each node, we have two status, whether to $\oplus$ k or not. Therefore, the definition of the DP array will be: $dp[i][0/1]$ means whether there are odd (1) or even (0) $\oplus$ k operations when traversing to the ith node.
We then have the formular:
When this node $\oplus$ with k: $dp[i][0] = max(dp[i-1][0] + nums[i], dp[i-1][1] + (nums[i] ^ k))$ When this node do not $\oplus$ with k: $dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1] + nums[i], dp[i-1][0] + (nums[i] ^ k))$ Note that there are always even $\oplus$ operations, so the answer would be $dp[n-1][0]$.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp = [[0 for _ in range(2)] for _ in range(n)] dp[0][0] = nums[0] dp[0][1] = (nums[0] ^ k) for i in range(1, n): dp[i][0] = max(dp[i-1][0] + nums[i], dp[i-1][1] + (nums[i] ^ k)) dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][0] + (nums[i] ^ k), dp[i-1][1] + nums[i]) return dp[-1][0] Method 4: DP without tree with better memory Intuition and Approach Same as Method 2, we also find out that the dp[i] formular only use twice. In this case, we can use two variables instead of the whold array to have a better memory usage.
Also, the $max$ operations is too slow in python, so a better way is to use if else equations instead of max.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp0, dp1 = 0, -10_000_000_000 for i in range(n): a = nums[i] b = a ^ k new_dp0 = dp0 + a if dp0 + a > dp1 + b else dp1 + b new_dp1 = dp0 + b if dp0 + b > dp1 + a else dp1 + a dp0, dp1 = new_dp0, new_dp1 return dp0 Method 5: Greedy algorithm Intuition and Approach Another way to look at this method without of tree is using greedy algorithm. Because we know that we can $\oplus$ k as long as we can find a pair of nodes, we can use greedy algorithm to find the pairs that has the most differences after $\oplus$ k.
That is, we can first $\oplus$ every element with k, calculating the difference between the new array and the previous array, then find all the pairs that has a difference that is larger than zero, then we get our answer.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: ans = sum(nums) diff = [(x ^ k) - x for x in nums] cnt,l,r = 0,inf,-inf for x in diff: if x > 0: cnt += 1 if x < l: l = x ans += x else: if r < x: r = x if cnt % 2 == 1: ans += max(-l, r) return ans I don’t know why using sort to do greedy algorithm is so neat and fast. Just as the one in the official solution.

content: class Solution: def colorTheGrid(self, m: int, n: int) -> int: pat = [] col = [0, 1, 2] def dfs(x, s): if x == m: pat.append(s) return for i in col: if x == 0 or s[x - 1] != i: dfs(x + 1, s + [i]) dfs(0, []) # till this step, we find all valid patterns for a column and store it in the pattern list. l = len(pat) valid = [[True for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(l)] for i in range(l): for j in range(i + 1, l): for k in range(m): if pat[i][k] == pat[j][k]: valid[i][j] = False break # till this step, we find all the pattern pairs that is valid. dp = [[0 for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(n)] mod = 1_000_000_007 for i in range(l): dp[0][i] = 1 # for column 0, each pattern is valid. for i in range(1, n): for x in range(l): for y in range(x + 1, l): if valid[x][y]: dp[i][x] = (dp[i][x] + dp[i-1][y]) % mod dp[i][y] = (dp[i][y] + dp[i-1][x]) % mod # we elaborate to the next column according to the DP formula. ans = 0 for i in range(l): ans = (ans + dp[-1][i]) % mod # finally, we add up all the answers. return ans ```]

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/letter-tile-possibilities/description/
Intuition In this question, we want to know how many different tiles we can generate.
Approach Therefore, we can use backtracking to enumerate all the solutions.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(2^N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Code class Solution: def numTilePossibilities(self, tiles: str) -> int: counter = defaultdict(int) for ch in tiles: counter[ch] += 1 def dfs(counter): total = 0 for ch in counter: if counter[ch] == 0: continue # Choose character total += 1 counter[ch] -= 1 total += dfs(counter) counter[ch] += 1 # backtracking return total return dfs(counter)

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/check-if-grid-can-be-cut-into-sections
Intuition This question asks about merging sections. In this case, if we smash it into 1D array, it just means “Is there more than two gaps inside the section?”
Approach Therefore, we can sort the list, and then iterate the whole list and see whether there is a gap between the section we already iterated and the new section. If there is, then add 1, else, merge this new section to our old section. The thing is that you need to do it twice.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of rectangles. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def checkValidCuts(self, n: int, rectangles: List[List[int]]) -> bool: N = len(rectangles) def get_res(a,b): rectangles.sort(key = lambda x: (x[a], x[b])) gapCnt,maxPos,l = 0,1,0 while(l < N): while(l < N and rectangles[l][a] < maxPos): maxPos = max(maxPos, rectangles[l][b]) l += 1 if l == N: break else: gapCnt += 1 maxPos = rectangles[l][b] # print(a,l) if gapCnt > 1: return True return False return get_res(0,2) or get_res(1,3)
summary: 1. Check if Grid can be Cut into Sections
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-03-25

content:
Intuition In this question, we find that if we flip a coin twice, then it is the same as flipping it zero times. Moreover, the only three ways to flip a coin is: Flip this coin Flip the coin before Flip the coin before this coin This means that if we pass this coin, we can no longer flip this coin. Approach In this case, we can iterate from front to end and flip every coin that is 0, and check whether the whole array is 1 at the end. Why is this method correct then? First we know that we cannot flip a coin after we pass this coin.
This means that we must flip this coin. If we do not flip this coin, then this coin will remain 0, which does not satisfy the quetion. Therefore, this step is required, missing this step will not give us the array we need.
We can also prove that this will lead us to the result for every array that can achieve this step. Because you have to flip this coin no matter what operation you did.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: cnt = 0 for i in range(len(nums) - 2): if nums[i] == 0: cnt += 1 nums[i] = 1 nums[i + 1] = 1 - nums[i + 1] nums[i + 2] = 1 - nums[i + 2] if nums[-1] == 1 and nums[-2] == 1: return cnt return -1
summary: 1. Minimum Operations to Make Binary Array Elements Equal to One I

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/product-of-the-last-k-numbers/description/
Intuition Situation 1: if there is no 0: In this case, we can just use a prefix multiplication to do what the question asks. Situation 2: if there is 0: Then it would be 0! Therefore, all we need is to just check whether there is a 0 in the last k numbers of the stream. If yes, them just return 0.
Approach Use an array to store the multiplication prefix. Check whether there is a zero in the last k streams. Complexity Time complexity: $O(Q)$, Q means the number of operations.
Space complexity: $O(Q)$, Q means the number of operations.
Code class ProductOfNumbers: def __init__(self): self.q = [] self.mul = 1 def add(self, num: int) -> None: self.mul *= num self.q.append(self.mul) if num == 0: self.q = [] self.mul = 1 def getProduct(self, k: int) -> int: if k > len(self.q): return 0 if k == len(self.q): return self.mul return self.mul // self.q[-k - 1] # Your ProductOfNumbers object will be instantiated and called as such: # obj = ProductOfNumbers() # obj.add(num) # param_2 = obj.getProduct(k)
summary: 1. Product of the Last K Numbers

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/design-a-number-container-system/description/
Intuition In this question, we need to find the smallest index of the number in the list. Note that the index may change when dealing with the list. Because we need the smallest index, so we need a sorted datastructure. Trick: We can get a lazy tag that stores the number of each index, so that we can pop only when we find our current answer does not fullfill the requirement.
Approach Store a dictionary that stores the sorted sequence of the numbers and the index. Store a dictionary of index and numbers pair. Check whether the answer is valid in the find function. Complexity Time complexity: $O(Q\ times log(N))$, Q is the time of query, N is the size of the dictionary.
Space complexity: $O(Q)$, Q is the time of query.
Code class NumberContainers: def __init__(self): self.lst = dict() self.idx = dict() def change(self, index: int, number: int) -> None: if number not in self.lst: self.lst.update({number: []}) heappush(self.lst[number], index) self.idx.update({index: number}) def find(self, number: int) -> int: if number not in self.lst: return -1 while self.lst[number]: currIndex = self.lst[number][0] if self.idx[currIndex] != number: heappop(self.lst[number]) else: return currIndex return -1 # Your NumberContainers object will be instantiated and called as such: # obj = NumberContainers() # obj.change(index,number) # param_2 = obj.find(number)
summary: 1. Design a Number Container System

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/redundant-connection/description/
Intuition Note that in a ring, every node have more than one index. Therefore, if we delete all the index that has one index, the remaining points will be a ring.
Approach Note that the label starts from 1 and ends at n, you would like to decrease index by one.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$
Space complexity: $O(N)$
Code class Solution: def findRedundantConnection(self, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: N = len(edges) du = [0] * (N) E = [[] for _ in range(N)] for x,y in edges: x -= 1 y -= 1 du[x] += 1 du[y] += 1 E[x].append(y) E[y].append(x) q = deque([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] == 1: q.append(i) # print(du) while(len(q) > 0): x = q.popleft() du[x] = 0 for to in E[x]: if du[to] > 0: du[to] -= 1 if du[to] == 1: q.append(to) # print(du) loop = set([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] > 0: loop.add(i) for i in range(N - 1, -1, -1): x,y = edges[i] if x - 1 in loop and y - 1 in loop: return [x, y] return None
summary: 1. Redundant Connection

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/make-lexicographically-smallest-array-by-swapping-elements/description/
Intuition In this question, we are doing a sorting process that only difference less than limit can swap. Therefore, there forms “groups”. In a group, two sequential number has a difference less than limit. In this case, if we sort the numbers in a group, then we are all done. Approach We need to get the groups. We need to sort the array first. Then if the difference between two numbers are bigger than limit, then it would belong to two different groups. Then we sort the result for each group. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times log(N))$, N is the length of the array.
Space complexity: $O(N)$
Code class Solution: def lexicographicallySmallestArray(self, nums: List[int], limit: int) -> List[int]: sorted_nums = [] for idx, x in enumerate(nums): sorted_nums.append((x, idx)) sorted_nums.sort() # First we sort the array groups = [] tmp = [] for i in range(len(nums)): if i > 0 and sorted_nums[i][0] - sorted_nums[i-1][0] > limit: tmp.sort() groups.append(tmp) tmp = [] tmp.append(sorted_nums[i][1]) tmp.sort() groups.append(tmp) # Then we form groups idx = 0 pos = 0 ans = [0] * len(nums) for i in range(len(nums)): if pos == len(groups[idx]): pos = 0 idx += 1 ans[groups[idx][pos]] = sorted_nums[i][0] pos += 1 # Then we sort the groups return ans
summary: 1. Make Lexicographically Smallest Array by Swapping Elements

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-cost-to-make-at-least-one-valid-path-in-a-grid/
Intuition Consider a question:
Here, there is a graph, each edge has a value 1 or 0, and you should travel from point 0 to point N, what is the shortest path?
In this case, you would quickly think of graph algorithms such as Dijkstra, SPFA, or even BFS.
But what if I tell you that the question above is the exact same question as what we are solving this quesion? Can you quickly think of the transition between the setting in the question and the setting in this more simple version?
Now, some of you may think that this problem may be DP question: we have a grid, we may be able to write a DP formular…
But wait, the most important prerequisite of DP is aftereffect. To run a DP, you must make sure that there is not aftereffect. In our situation, because we may need to go from right to left, from down to top, so aftereffect exists. Therefore, we cannot use DP in this question.
Approach Now, to transfer our question to the question above, we only need to iterate through the graph and create an edge between a point to its neighbor, if this is the neighbor it is pointing at, then the value of the edge will be 0, otherwise it would be 1.
Some of you may consern the correctness of this solution, as there is also a limitation that “You can modify the sign on a cell one time only”.
However, the situation is, this graph has not negative edges, which means that your result will always increase if you go through more points. Therefore, you will not even vist the same point more than 1 time, so it is impossible for the solution you get to change the sign of a cell more than 1 time.
Now, run your Dijkstra (or other shortest path algorithms), and you are all set!
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times M\times log(N\times M))$
Space complexity: $O(N\times M)$
Code class Solution: def minCost(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] n,m = len(grid), len(grid[0]) edges = [[] for i in range(n * m)] def cordinate2d21d(x,y): return x * m + y for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): pos = cordinate2d21d(i, j) for idx in range(4): nx = i + dx[idx] ny = j + dy[idx] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m: continue npos = cordinate2d21d(nx, ny) if idx + 1 == grid[i][j]: edges[pos].append([npos, 0]) else: edges[pos].append([npos, 1]) dis = [inf] * (n * m) dis[0] = 0 q = [(0,0)] while q: d, x = heappop(q) for to, v in edges[x]: if d + v < dis[to]: dis[to] = d + v heappush(q, (dis[to], to)) return dis[cordinate2d21d(n-1, m-1)]
summary: 1. Minimum Cost to Make at Least One Valid Path in a Grid
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-18

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/bitwise-xor-of-all-pairings/description/
Intuition According to the question, the formular of the output would be: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} nums1[i]\space xor \space nums2[j] $$ where $\large{XOR}$ means the operation that xor from $i$ to $n$. Here, $n$ means the length of $nums1$, and $m$ means the length of $nums2$. According to the properties of xor, xor satisfies the law of commutation and the law of association (more information can be seen here), so we can change the formular to: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} (nums1[i]^m) \space xor \space \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} (nums2[j]^n) $$ According to the property that $A\space xor A = 0$, we now know that the result would be: $$ ans = \large{XOR}{\small i = 1}^{\small n} (nums1[i]^{m % 2}) \space xor \space \large{XOR}{\small j = 1}^{\small m} (nums2[j]^{n % 2}) $$
Approach Iterate two arrays and apply the formular above.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N + M)$, N is the length of nums1, M is the length of nums2.
Space complexity: $O(1)$, no more space is used.
Code class Solution: def xorAllNums(self, nums1: List[int], nums2: List[int]) -> int: ans = 0 if len(nums1) % 2 == 1: for x in nums2: ans ^= x if len(nums2) % 2 == 1: for x in nums1: ans ^= x return ans
summary: 1. Bitwise XOR of All Pairings
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-16

Intuition In this question, the only three ways that the answer will increase one is:
$A[i] == B[i]$. $A[i]$ appears in $B$. $B[i]$ appears in $A$. Approach Iterate through the Array, then check for those three situation. Note that situation 1 conflicts with situation 2 & 3. That is, if cnt is add by 1 through situation 1, then situation 2 & 3 will not increase cnt. But situation 2 & 3 could increase cnt. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the list.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, because we need to store a set.
Code class Solution: def findThePrefixCommonArray(self, A: List[int], B: List[int]) -> List[int]: ans = [] cntA = set([]) cntB = set([]) cnt = 0 for i in range(len(A)): if A[i] == B[i]: cnt += 1 else: if A[i] in cntB: cnt += 1 if B[i] in cntA: cnt += 1 cntA.add(A[i]) cntB.add(B[i]) ans.append(cnt) return ans
summary: 1. Find the Prefix Common Array of Two Arrays

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/word-subsets/
Intuition The brute method would take $O(N^2\times (L+D))$ time, while $N$ is the length of the words, $L$ is the length of each word, and $D$ is the size of the dictionary (that is, 26 characters). However, $N \le 10^4$, which means that we cannot use brute method. Then we found out that we do not need to compare all $N$ words, instead, we only need to compare the maximum of the occurence of each characters in words2. For example, if ‘a’ appears 3 times in $words2[1]$, 2 times in $words2[2]$, 4 times in $words2[3]$, then ‘a’ must appears at least 4 times in a $words1[i]$ to add one to the answer. Therefore, all we need is to count the occurence of each character in word1, and count the maximun occurence of each character in every word2.
Approach Count the occurence of each character in word1, and count the maximun occurence of each character in every word2.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N \times (L + D))$, while $N$ is the length of the words, $L$ is the length of each word, and $D$ is the size of the dictionary (that is, 26 characters).
Space complexity: $O(N \ times D)$.
Code class Solution: def wordSubsets(self, words1: List[str], words2: List[str]) -> List[str]: wordcnt1, wordcnt2 = [], [0] * 26 a = ord('a') for word in words1: cnt = [0] * 26 for ch in word: x = ord(ch) - a cnt[x] += 1 wordcnt1.append(cnt) for word in words2: cnt = [0] * 26 for ch in word: x = ord(ch) - a cnt[x] += 1 for j in range(26): wordcnt2[j] = max(wordcnt2[j], cnt[j]) ans = [] for i in range(len(words1)): flag = True for k in range(26): if wordcnt1[i][k] < wordcnt2[k]: flag = False break if flag: ans.append(words1[i]) return ans
summary: 1. Word Subsets

content: Today’s Problem class Solution: def minOperations(self, boxes: str) -> List[int]: now = 0 cnt1 = 0 for i in range(len(boxes)): if boxes[i] == '1': now += i cnt1 += 1 ans = [now] * len(boxes) now_cnt1 = 0 for i in range(1, len(boxes)): if boxes[i-1] == '1': now_cnt1 += 1 ans[i] = ans[i-1] + 2 * now_cnt1 - cnt1 return ans
summary: 1. The minimum number of operations required to move all balls into each box.
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-06

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/shifting-letters-ii/description/
Intuition We need to write a datastructure to achieve a multi-range addition and a one-time query. Therefore, we can use difference Array and prefix sum.
Approach In a difference array, we only count the difference of the edges of the change zone. For example, if we add the range $[l,r]$ by $k$, we only pay attention to point l and point r: we add the difference array $diff[l]$ by $k$, and then add the difference array $diff[r + 1]$ by $-k$. Now, if we calculate the prefix sum $s$ in range $[l,r]$, we find that the effect of addition $k$ will be added only in range $[l,r]$ in $s$. Therefore, by using the difference array and the prefix sum, we can deal with the one change in $O(1)$. Now, since we want to change the character, we can first change it into ASCII code, then subtract by the code of ‘a’. Then we can use a module of 26 to acheive the effect of “character rotation”.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N + C)$, N is the length of the string, c is the number of changes.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, we need to store the difference array.
Code class Solution: def shiftingLetters(self, s: str, shifts: List[List[int]]) -> str: dif = [0] * (len(s) + 1) for l,r,delta in shifts: if delta == 0: dif[l] -= 1 dif[r + 1] += 1 else: dif[l] += 1 dif[r + 1] -= 1 a = ord('a') cnt = 0 ans = "" for i in range(len(s)): cnt += dif[i] ans += chr((ord(s[i]) - a + cnt) % 26 + a) return ans
summary: 1. Shifting Letters II
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-05

content:
Today’s Question https://leetcode.com/problems/unique-length-3-palindromic-subsequences/description/
Approach First, calculate the positions where each character first and last appears. Then, traverse between l and r, and count how many different characters there are among them.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(kN))$, k is the number of different characters, here it is 26 different letters, N is the length of the string.
Space complexity: $O(N)$
summary: 1. Different palindrome substrings with a length of 3.
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-04

content: class Solution: def waysToSplitArray(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: now,summ, ans = 0, 0, sum(nums) for num in nums[:-1]: now += num if now >= summ - now: ans += 1 return ans
summary: The number of solutions for splitting a set of elements.
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-03

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/count-vowel-strings-in-ranges/description/
Intuition We need to cacluate the sum of a section, while the sum of the any section remains constant for every query. Therefore we can use prefix sum.
Approach In one iteration, we can identify whether $words[i]$ is the a vowel string or not. In this case, we can apply a prefix sum to calculate the number of vowel strings between index 1 and index i. Therefore, when we want to calculate the number of vowel strings between index l and index r, we can just use $num(1\space to\space r) - num(1\space to\space l-1)$ as our result.
Trick In python, $list[-1]$ means the final index of the list. Therefore, we can add a [0] at the end of our prefix sum list to avoid null index.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(k\times N + Q)$, while k is the number of vowels, N is the length of the words, Q is the length of the queries.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, because we stored a new list.
Code class Solution: def vowelStrings(self, words: List[str], queries: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: sumWords = [0] * (len(words) + 1) vowels = set(['a','e','i','o','u']) for idx, word in enumerate(words): sumWords[idx] = sumWords[idx-1] if word[0] in vowels and word[-1] in vowels: sumWords[idx] += 1 ans = [] for x,y in queries: ans.append(sumWords[y] - sumWords[x-1]) return ans
summary: 1. Count Vowel Strings in Ranges
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-02

summary:
Llama 部分

Ollama 部分

首先，在服务器上启动一个 ollama 服务：

ml ollama # activate ollama
ollama serve # open ollama server

在 Python 中使用 ollama 的示例：

pip install ollama
from ollama import chat, Client, ChatResponse

client = Client(host='http://localhost:11434')
model_use = "llama3.2"

completion = client.chat(
    model=model_use,
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Extract the event information."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Alice and Bob are going to a science fair on Friday."},
    ],
)

completion['message']['content']
```]

这个 ollama 服务主要用于获取与 ChatGPT 相关的信息。
title: LLM Study

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-score-after-splitting-a-string/description
Intuition Do what the question asks.
Approach Do what the question asks. Iterate from the back to the front to count how many 1s, iterate from the front to the back to count how many 2s. To speed up the process, we can first count how many 1s are there in the whole sequence, then use another iteration to count the remaining 1s in the sequence by doing a subtraction. Same to the question in Jan.03.2025, the number of 1s in index i + 1 to n = the number of 1s in the sequence - the number of 1s in index 1 to i.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of s.
Space complexity: $O(1)$, only a few new variables are stored.
Code class Solution: def maxScore(self, s: str) -> int: num1 = 0 for ch in s: if ch == '1': num1 += 1 now0, now1, ans = 0, 0, 0 for ch in s[:-1]: if ch == '0': now0 += 1 else: now1 += 1 ans = max(ans, now0 + num1 - now1) return ans
summary: 1. Maximum Score After Splitting a String
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-01
